findings.description: Unlike every other spec in this workstream (whose declared Primary CTA matches the button actually shown in that screen's default/entry state — e.g. dashboard.md's 'Start review', export.md's 'Export'), deck-settings.md's declared Primary CTA of 'Save' does not correspond to anything visible in the screen's actual default state, which is a peer-action menu with no single primary action.
findings.impact: Low — cosmetic/documentation clarity only; could mislead a reader skimming just the spec's Objective/Primary-CTA line into thinking Deck Settings opens directly into a save-able form rather than an action-sheet menu.
findings.recommendation: Either state "Primary CTA: none — an action-sheet menu of five peer actions" (matching add-card-target.md's pattern for a similar no-single-CTA surface) or clarify that 'Save' refers specifically to the nested rename sub-flow.

findings.title: MxIconButton's REQUIRED ariaLabel prop is omitted across most icon-only buttons in this feature cluster
findings.evidenceA: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/components/navigation/MxIconButton.d.ts:10-16 — "Accessible label — REQUIRED. The button has no visible text; a screen reader must not fall back to the raw icon ligature (e.g. \"arrow_back\", \"more_horiz\")."; implementation fallback at MxIconButton.jsx:10 `aria-label={ariaLabel || icon}`.
findings.evidenceB: reminder/Reminder.jsx:14 `<MxIconButton icon="arrow_back" node="reminder/back" />` and :50 `<MxIconButton icon="schedule" node="reminder/time-edit" />`; review-mode/ReviewMode.jsx:14-16 (back/text-size/options) and :62,64 (prev/next); review-mode/components/MeaningCard.jsx:11 (edit/close toggle); settings/Settings.jsx:43 (Child helper's back button, used by 5 child screens) and :62 (study-hub back); theme/Theme.jsx:18 (back); study-session/StudySession.jsx:54,56 (close/options); study-session/components/ResumeErrorState.jsx:10 (close) — none of these pass ariaLabel.
findings.description: MxIconButton.d.ts marks ariaLabel REQUIRED and the component itself only falls back to the raw Material Symbols ligature string when it's missing, which is exactly the failure mode the doc comment says must never happen. In this feature-group cluster the vast majority of MxIconButton call sites (back buttons, close, more-options, prev/next, edit/close toggle, schedule edit) omit ariaLabel entirely, while the sibling Search and Subdeck-list features in the same kit consistently supply it (e.g. search/Search.jsx:24 ariaLabel="Clear"), proving this is not a hard technical constraint but an inconsistently-applied requirement.
findings.impact: A screen-reader user navigating Reminders, Review, Settings (root + all 5 child screens), Study Session, or Theme will hear meaningless raw glyph names like "arrow back", "more vert", "chevron left", "schedule", "close" instead of actionable labels like "Back", "More options", "Next card", "Edit reminder time", "Close" — a serious, broad accessibility regression affecting nearly every screen in this workstream's cluster.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit ariaLabel to every MxIconButton instance in these files (mirroring the pattern already used correctly in search/ and subdeck-list/), and add a lint/verify step (or extend the existing design-checklist validator) that fails when MxIconButton is used without ariaLabel, since the current structural validator evidently does not catch this.

findings.title: Theme AccentPicker swatch buttons have no accessible name at all
findings.evidenceA: theme/components/AccentPicker.jsx:11 `` — no aria-label and no visible text; the accessible content is empty for every unselected swatch.
findings.evidenceB: theme/components/AccentPicker.d.ts:1-13 documents only swatches and accent props, with no note that Flutter must supply a semantic/color label — unlike reminder/TimeCol.d.ts and reminder/TimePickerSheet.d.ts which explicitly call out accessibility follow-ups ("an accessible semanticLabel is added — recorded as exceptions").
findings.description: Unlike MxIconButton, the raw <button> used for each accent swatch carries neither a visible label nor an aria-label; for non-selected swatches there is no text node at all (the checkmark <span> only renders for the selected swatch). This is worse than the MxIconButton gap above because there isn't even an icon-ligature fallback to read.
findings.impact: A screen-reader user cannot tell which color each swatch button represents or which one is currently selected (other than the one lone check glyph, itself unlabeled) — the accent-color picker is effectively unusable non-visually.
findings.recommendation: Add aria-label (e.g. "Indigo", "Violet", ...) per swatch and aria-pressed/aria-checked semantics for the selected state; document the required prop in AccentPicker.d.ts the same way TimeCol.d.ts documents its Flutter accessibility exception.

findings.title: Autogenerated composition-map bar-variant claims are factually wrong in 2 of 9 reviewed specs
findings.evidenceA: specs/study-session.md:19 "Top → bottom: **ghost MxContextualAppBar** → primary content → primary action (Next)."
findings.evidenceB: study-session/StudySession.jsx:53 `<MxContextualAppBar variant="nested" node="study-session/appbar" ...>` (also study-session/components/ResumeErrorState.jsx:9, same variant="nested"); there is no variant="ghost" anywhere in the MxContextualAppBar usages across the kit — "ghost" only exists as an MxButton variant elsewhere in the same source file (e.g. study-session/components/StageFill.jsx:13). Separately, specs/settings.md:19 says "nested MxContextualAppBar" but Settings.jsx:125 (the primary/default `loaded` state) uses `variant="root"` with a bottom nav; "nested" only applies to the 6 child sub-screens reached from it.
findings.description: specs/study-session.md and specs/settings.md both open with "AUTOGENERATED... Source of truth: .../<Feature>.jsx" yet their generated composition-map sentence names an app-bar variant that does not match the source's primary/default state. This looks like a generator bug (possibly conflating the "ghost" MxButton variant string with the app-bar variant for study-session, and defaulting to the child-screen variant for settings).
findings.impact: Anyone implementing the Flutter screen from the generated spec text alone (without also reading the .jsx, which the spec says is only needed for pixel composition) will build the wrong app-bar chrome for the primary state of both screens.
findings.recommendation: Fix the registry/generator (tool/ui_kit_shots/registry.mjs) that derives the composition-map bar-variant text, then regenerate specs/study-session.md and specs/settings.md (and audit the other 25 registry-generated specs for the same class of error).

findings.title: study-result.md's declared Primary CTA text never appears in the actual source
findings.evidenceA: specs/study-result.md:11 "Primary CTA: **Continue studying**"
findings.evidenceB: study-result/components/Cta.jsx:9 `<MxButton variant="primary" icon="bolt" block node="study-result/continue">Keep studying</MxButton>` (standard/goal-met default case); :15 "Review 8 cards" (many-wrong); :20 "Keep going" (goal-missed) — the string "Continue studying" does not occur anywhere in StudyResult.jsx or its Cta.jsx.
findings.description: The autogenerated Primary CTA label doesn't match any of the three actual button labels the Cta component renders across its states, even though the spec header states it is auto-derived from the same source file it disagrees with.
findings.impact: Minor but real: a reader relying on the spec text (rather than the source or PNGs) for copy would ship the wrong button label.
findings.recommendation: Regenerate/correct the Primary CTA field to read "Keep studying" (the base-state label), or make the generator pull the literal button text instead of a paraphrase.

findings.title: subdeck-list feature lacks the spec/registry/prop-parity artifacts every sibling feature has
findings.evidenceA: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/INDEX.md:9-38 lists all 27 registry-tracked screens (reminder, review-mode, search, settings, statistics, study-result, study-session, theme, library, ...) — subdeck-list is not among them, and there is no specs/subdeck-list.md.
findings.evidenceB: _features/subdeck-list/ contains SubdeckList.jsx, components/CreateDeckSheet.jsx and components/subdeckFixtures.jsx but, unlike every other _features/* folder in this review (reminder, review-mode, search, settings, statistics, study-result, study-session, theme all ship a *.card.html gallery entry and a .d.ts for every feature-local composite such as TimePickerSheet.d.ts, ValuePickerSheet.d.ts, MeaningCard.d.ts, Chips.d.ts, ExitDialog.d.ts), subdeck-list has no .card.html at all and no .d.ts for CreateDeckSheet.jsx.
findings.description: SubdeckList.jsx is genuinely load-bearing (library.md's component map explicitly lists window.SubdeckList and it backs 15 of Library's 31 nested-* states per subdeck-list/SubdeckList.jsx:1-7's own header comment), so its absence from the registry/INDEX pipeline is defensible as "it's not an independently-routed top-level screen" — but the missing .d.ts for CreateDeckSheet.jsx (a bottom-sheet composite directly analogous to TimePickerSheet/ValuePickerSheet, both of which do have .d.ts files) breaks the otherwise-universal convention this review observed across all 8 sibling directories, with no documented reason.
findings.impact: Flutter implementers have no fixture-parameterized-exception documentation for CreateDeckSheet's props/parity (whether it should even be built — see C8-006), unlike every comparable sheet in the kit.
findings.recommendation: Either add components/CreateDeckSheet.d.ts following the established pattern, or explicitly document in the subdeck-list folder (and/or subdeckFixtures.jsx) why this composite is exempt from the convention.

findings.title: CreateDeckSheet.jsx is dead/unused fixture code with only an inline comment as justification
findings.evidenceA: subdeck-list/SubdeckList.jsx:19 destructures CreateDeckSheet from window.MemoXSubdeckList but never renders it; the create-sheet state instead returns window.CreateDeckDialog({ state: 'nested' }) at SubdeckList.jsx:138, with the inline comment: "create → Create Deck DIALOG with this parent as context (§13); no nested create sheet. CreateDeckSheet.jsx is kept as a component/node map for the app's current build."
findings.evidenceB: subdeck-list/components/CreateDeckSheet.jsx:1-4 header comment: "CreateDeckSheet — the create sheet for the nested-deck list screen... (Node ids keep the frozen subdeck-list/… contract.)" — no d.ts, spec, or exceptions file cross-references this note or explains for whom/what the "node map" is being kept.
findings.description: The file is confirmed unreachable from any SubdeckList state, yet its header comment implies it still matters ("kept... for the app's current build") without saying whether Flutter should implement it, ignore it, or treat its subdeck-list/create-* node ids as reserved-but-unused.
findings.impact: An implementer skimming the components/ folder (rather than tracing SubdeckList.jsx's actual render logic) could reasonably build a CreateDeckSheet widget that the product no longer uses, wasting effort, or could delete node ids that some other doc still depends on.
findings.recommendation: Either delete CreateDeckSheet.jsx if truly superseded by CreateDeckDialog, or add a components/CreateDeckSheet.d.ts / spec note explicitly stating its status (e.g. "retained for node-id history only; do not implement").

findings.title: Stale state-list header comments undercount actual states in 3 screen source files
findings.evidenceA: reminder/Reminder.jsx:1 "States: on · off · time-picker" (3 named) vs. actual switch on state supporting 5: on, off, time-picker, permission-denied, permission-denied-expansion (Reminder.jsx:12-73, matches specs/reminder.md's 5-state matrix).
findings.evidenceB: review-mode/ReviewMode.jsx:1 "States: browsing · editing · audio · end" (4 named) vs. actual 6: browsing, editing, audio, loading, error, end (ReviewMode.jsx:8-68, matches specs/review-mode.md's 6-state matrix); statistics/Statistics.jsx:1 "States: loading · loaded · insufficient · scope-switch" (4 named) vs. actual 5, missing error (Statistics.jsx:16-93, matches specs/statistics.md's 5-state matrix).
findings.description: These are header comments inside the .jsx source itself (not the generated .md specs, which are all correct) — a lower-severity but real drift risk since the comment is the first thing a reader sees when opening the file.
findings.impact: Low — the generated specs and INDEX.md are accurate, so this only misleads someone reading the raw source comment as the state inventory (e.g. missing that Reminders has a permission-denied recovery state, or that Review/Statistics have loading/error states).
findings.recommendation: Update the three header comments to list the full state set, or better, have them simply point to the generated spec's state matrix instead of duplicating a list that can drift.

findings.title: settings.md is hand-edited, breaking the "do not hand-edit" autogeneration contract the other 8 specs in this cluster follow
findings.evidenceA: specs/settings.md:3-4 "> Rebaselined 2026-07-18 from ../_features/settings/Settings.jsx. Historical PNGs are compact references; SRS and Tier-1 adaptive evidence must be refreshed before sign-off." — no "AUTOGENERATED... do not hand-edit" banner, no "Verify: node tool/ui_kit_shots/shoot.mjs settings" pointer.
findings.evidenceB: Every other spec in this cluster (reminder.md:3, review-mode.md:3, search.md:3, statistics.md:3, study-result.md:3, study-session.md:3, theme.md:3) opens with the identical standard banner "> AUTOGENERATED from tool/ui_kit_shots/registry.mjs — do not hand-edit; run npm run gen:ui-kit." and specs/INDEX.md:7 states "This index can never again claim a screen the registry doesn't have" implying every listed spec (including settings, INDEX.md:37) is registry-generated.
findings.description: settings.md was manually rewritten (dated exactly today, 2026-07-18) outside the stated generation pipeline while still being listed in INDEX.md as a registry-tracked spec, and it self-reports that its own evidence (PNGs, SRS/Tier-1 adaptive proof) is stale/unrefreshed and not yet signed off — a caveat worth weighing against any external claim that Settings/SRS documentation is fully "Closed" (cf. docs/audits/documentation-remediation-2026-07-18.md lines 23/36, which characterize the SRS/read-only contract and business/design conflicts as accepted/"Closed contractually" at a summary level; that report does separately acknowledge 11 PARTIAL groups and 13 open P1 items, so this is not a flat contradiction, but settings.md's own text is the clearest first-hand admission that this particular spec's evidence is not release-ready).
findings.impact: Low/process risk: the pipeline invariant ("never hand-edit generated specs") is silently broken for exactly one file, which could cause the next npm run gen:ui-kit run to silently overwrite this hand-added caveat, losing the "must be refreshed before sign-off" warning.
findings.recommendation: Either fold the SRS-evidence caveat into the registry (tool/ui_kit_shots/registry.mjs) so it survives regeneration, or move it out of settings.md into a tracked issue-register entry, restoring the standard banner.

findings.title: Remove Language Pair: design implements cascading delete of decks/cards; business explicitly forbids cascade delete and requires blocking on dependency
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/language-pair/remove-language-pair.md:7-8 "Pair có Deck dependency không được xóa trực tiếp. Không cascade delete Deck/Card." and line 19 flowchart branch "C -- 'Có' [Has Deck dependencies] --> D['Block · list/manage Decks']", and line 47 acceptance criterion "Không có orphan Deck hoặc cascade delete."
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/languages/components/RemoveLanguageDialog.jsx:9-16 — title "Remove 한국어 → English?" body text "All decks and cards for this pair will be deleted. This can't be undone." with only Cancel/Remove actions; Languages.jsx has no 'blocked-by-dependency' state, and the languages.md spec's 6-state matrix (list/one/empty/add/remove/scripts) never surfaces a dependency-blocked screen.
findings.description: Business mandates that a language pair with any dependent Deck cannot be removed directly — the UI must block removal and show/manage the dependent decks instead, and cascade-deleting decks/cards is explicitly prohibited as an acceptance criterion. The design kit's RemoveLanguageDialog does the opposite: it always presents removal as an immediate destructive action that deletes 'All decks and cards for this pair', with no dependency check, no blocked state, and no path to review/manage the dependent decks first.
findings.impact: If implemented as designed, a user could permanently delete an entire deck hierarchy and all its learning progress by removing a language pair — a severe, irreversible data-loss path the business spec was written specifically to prevent.
findings.recommendation: Product Owner must decide the real removal UX: either (a) the design's cascading-delete confirm is correct and remove-language-pair.md's no-cascade/dependency-block rule is stale and should be revised, or (b) the design must add a dependency-check + blocked state (per business) before removal is ever offered, and the confirm copy must stop claiming decks/cards will be deleted.

findings.title: Duplicate-flashcard resolution: design offers only 'View existing' / 'Add anyway'; business requires Edit / Open existing / Keep both / Merge with a side-by-side comparison
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/flashcard/resolve-duplicate-flashcard.md:16-20 entry-cases table row "Create draft | Existing Card | Edit draft, open existing, keep both, merge draft fields"; §4 template lines 42-49: "This card may already exist / New / edited card    Existing card / <term · meaning>    <term · meaning> / Review the differences before saving."; §5 (lines 51-58) separately defines Edit, Open existing, Keep both, Merge as distinct decisions.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/flashcard-editor/components/DupBanner.jsx:9-22 — the entire duplicate UI is a single-line warning ("A card '안녕하세요' already exists in this deck.") with exactly two buttons, 'View existing' and 'Add anyway'; there is no rendered comparison of the new/draft card vs the existing card, and no Merge action anywhere in the flashcard-editor feature.
findings.description: Business defines duplicate resolution as a compare/decision flow with four distinct outcomes (Edit, Open existing, Keep both, Merge) and an explicit side-by-side content comparison. The design's DupBanner collapses this to a two-button warning strip with no comparison view and no Merge capability at all.
findings.impact: Users cannot merge duplicate cards from the editor per the design as built, and cannot see what the 'existing' card actually contains before deciding — increasing the chance of accidental true-duplicate creation or missed legitimate merges, contradicting an explicit acceptance-parity requirement in resolve-duplicate-flashcard.md §8.
findings.recommendation: Product Owner must decide whether Merge and the comparison view are in scope for the MVP dup-resolution UI; if yes, the design kit needs a new dup-resolution screen/state, if no, resolve-duplicate-flashcard.md must be narrowed to match the simpler two-action model the design implements.

findings.title: Card audio: design collapses to auto-generate + play only; business requires Attach file and Remove as first-class actions with their own state matrix
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/flashcard/manage-card-audio.md §4 composition (lines 45-50): "Audio / <voice/language>    Play / Generate audio   Attach file   Remove"; §9 State matrix (lines 81-86) lists 'Attach valid/invalid', 'regenerate', 'remove' and 'version conflict' as required states distinct from generation.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/flashcard-editor/components/AudioRow.jsx:1-6 header comment: "The pronunciation control, collapsed from a full-width row into a COMPACT icon button... Pronunciation is auto-generated from the term, so it no longer needs its own section — a tap plays it; no autoplay." The component's only states are auto/ready/generating/error (lines 12-17) — there is no Attach-file affordance and no Remove affordance anywhere in the flashcard-editor feature.
findings.description: The design explicitly documents a deliberate simplification: the audio row was reduced from a full section to a single icon button that can only auto-generate and play. Business's manage-card-audio.md treats Attach file and Remove as equally weighted, independently-stated actions alongside Generate, with a much larger required state matrix.
findings.impact: If the business contract stands, users have no way to attach a pre-recorded/uploaded audio file or remove an existing audio reference from the card editor as currently designed — a documented capability is entirely absent from the mock.
findings.recommendation: Product Owner must confirm whether Attach-file/Remove are in scope for v1; if yes, AudioRow needs redesign to expose them; if no, manage-card-audio.md must be revised to reflect the auto-generate-only model actually built.

findings.title: Additional translations: design supports exactly one extra translation; business requires an ordered, reorderable, multi-item list
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/flashcard/manage-card-translations.md:8 "Additional translation có stable identity và explicit order."; §4 composition (lines 41-48) shows a numbered list of at least two rows ("[ 1  Nghĩa bổ sung ... More ] [ 2  Another meaning ... More ] Add translation"); §5 (line 57) "Reorder uses accessible move actions in addition to gesture when applicable."
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/flashcard-editor/FlashcardEditor.jsx:20-27 defines a single `DECK.alt` language, and lines 182-187 render at most one 'additional-translation' Field with only a Remove action — there is no 'Add another translation', no numbering, and no reorder control; the 10-state matrix in flashcard-editor.md has a single `additional-translation` state, not a list-management state.
findings.description: Business models additional translations as an ordered collection supporting add/edit/remove/reorder of multiple entries, while the design's FlashcardEditor hard-codes a single `alt` language slot per deck and only ever shows/edits one translation field.
findings.impact: A card that needs two or more secondary translations cannot be modeled by the current editor; the business acceptance criterion 'additional entries nonblank/unique/ordered' (manage-card-translations.md line 82) cannot be satisfied by a single-slot UI.
findings.recommendation: Product Owner must decide whether multi-translation support is required for v1 (deck's alt-language model would need to become a list) or whether the business spec should be narrowed to the single-secondary-translation model actually designed.

findings.title: Import duplicate handling: design shows one generic 'import anyway?' banner; business requires per-item/bulk Skip / Merge / Import-anyway decisions with incoming-vs-existing comparison
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/content-transfer/resolve-import-duplicates.md:5 "Flow này tạo Skip, Merge hoặc Import anyway decision cho duplicate candidates trước commit."; §3 (line 33) "Incoming/existing content và Deck path phải phân biệt rõ."; §5 state matrix (lines 44-46) lists 'Skip/merge/keep-both/bulk/undo'.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/import/Import.jsx:113-115 — the entire dup-warning UI is `<window.ActionCallout ... text="8 cards already exist — import anyway?" />` layered over the same flat preview Table; there is no Skip/Merge/per-item resolution list, no incoming-vs-existing comparison, and the import.md spec's 8-state matrix has a single `dup-warning` state, not a resolution list archetype.
findings.description: Business defines duplicate handling during import as its own 'Compare/resolution list' screen with per-item and bulk Skip/Merge/Import-anyway decisions. The design kit reduces this to a single warning banner with an implicit all-or-nothing 'import anyway' continuation; consistent with this, commit-import.md's requirement for a created/merged/skipped/failed breakdown is never represented (the 'done' state only reports a single "Imported 124 cards" count, Import.jsx:51).
findings.impact: Users importing a file with duplicate candidates have no way to selectively skip or merge specific rows — only an all-or-nothing continue — which contradicts the business acceptance criterion that 'Không candidate nào bị overwrite không explicit' (resolve-import-duplicates.md line 50).
findings.recommendation: Product Owner must decide whether the richer per-item Skip/Merge resolution list is required for v1, and if so the import feature needs a new resolution-list state/screen; otherwise resolve-import-duplicates.md and commit-import.md should be narrowed to the single warning-and-continue model actually designed.

findings.title: Guess mode: business mandates round-complete / retry-round / invalid-distractor-pool as distinct states for the mastery-round loop; none exist in the design kit
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/study-mode/guess-card-meaning.md:61 (§5 State matrix) "Loading, waiting-five-options, correct, wrong, round-complete, retry-round, invalid-distractor-pool." and docs/business/study-mode/README.md:76 "Guess waiting-five-options/correct/wrong/invalid-distractor-pool/round-complete/retry-round/long-text/complete." Business also requires (guess-card-meaning.md:14) "Thiếu bốn distractor hợp lệ là question/session validation error: không render ít hơn năm lựa chọn..." — a fail-closed state when the distractor pool is inadequate.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/guess-mode.md:27-35 lists only 5 states (waiting, correct, wrong, long-text, complete); GuessMode.jsx has exactly one non-`waiting` branch (`state === 'complete'`, lines 32-40) and no code path at all for round-complete, retry-round, or invalid-distractor-pool.
findings.description: The business mastery-round contract requires an unbounded retry loop per graded mode: a round can complete with failed cards, which must start a new round from just those cards, distinguishable from full mode completion via a round-complete/retry-round transition. Guess also requires a fail-closed 'invalid-distractor-pool' state. The design kit has none of these — its only non-default screen is a single 'complete' state.
findings.impact: The mastery-round retry mechanic — a core behavior of every graded study mode per business — has zero UI representation to build against.
findings.recommendation: Product Owner/Design must decide whether round-complete/retry-round/invalid-distractor-pool need dedicated screens and commission them, or explicitly document that the mastery loop is meant to be invisible/instantaneous between rounds and update guess-card-meaning.md's state matrix accordingly.

findings.title: Match mode: business mandates round-complete / retry-round as distinct states for the mastery-round loop; not present in the design kit
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/study-mode/match-terms-and-meanings.md:68 (§5 State matrix) "Idle, one selected, correct, wrong, almost, round-complete, retry-round, complete." and the worked example table (lines 55-64) showing up to 4+ successive rounds for a single 10-card stage.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/match-mode.md:27-36 lists only 6 states (playing, selected, correct, wrong, almost, complete) — no round-complete/retry-round; MatchMode.jsx has only one non-default branch (`state === 'complete'`, lines 31-40).
findings.description: Same gap pattern as Guess (C7-006): business's Match mastery-round loop can run an unbounded number of rounds and requires the UI to distinguish 'this round is done, starting a retry round with only your missed pairs' from true mode completion; the design kit has no screen for the former.
findings.impact: Same as C7-006, applied to Match: the retry-round transition central to the graded-mode mechanic has no reference implementation.
findings.recommendation: Same decision needed as C7-006 for Match specifically — Product Owner/Design to commission round-complete/retry-round states or revise match-terms-and-meanings.md's state matrix.

findings.title: Recall mode: business mandates round-complete/retry-round plus countdown-10s/5s, background/resume and deadline-race states; design has only 6 flat states
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/study-mode/recall-and-self-grade.md §6 State matrix (lines 75-81): "Countdown-20s, countdown-10s, countdown-5s, manual-revealed, timeout-revealed, remembered, forgot-explicit, forgot-timeout. Background/paused timer, resume-with-remaining-time, deadline race, timeout save failure/retry. Round-complete, retry-round, submitting/failure." §Countdown accessibility (lines 69-73) requires announcing the 10s/5s thresholds distinctly.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/recall-mode.md:26-35 lists only 6 states (before-reveal, revealed, forgot, remembered, timed-out, complete); RecallMode.jsx has a static badge showing only 'Time: 00:20' or 'Time: 00:00' (lines 36-39) with no 10s/5s intermediate representation, and no round-complete/retry-round/submitting-failure/background-resume states anywhere.
findings.description: Business defines Recall's countdown and mastery-loop behavior with far more granularity than the design kit captures: distinct 10-second and 5-second screen-reader announcement thresholds, a background/paused/resume-with-remaining-time state, a deadline-race resolution state, and round-complete/retry-round/submitting-failure states for the mastery loop. None of these appear in the design kit's state matrix or JSX.
findings.impact: Beyond the shared mastery-loop gap (see C7-006/007), Recall additionally lacks any design reference for the 10s/5s accessibility announcement thresholds and the background/interruption-resume behavior that business calls out as acceptance criteria.
findings.recommendation: Product Owner/Design to decide which of these states need dedicated visual references (round-complete/retry-round almost certainly do) versus which are purely non-visual timer/accessibility behavior implementable from prose alone; update recall-and-self-grade.md or commission the missing screens accordingly.

findings.title: 6 of 10 specs in this group (21 of 29 kit-wide) state a 44×44 minimum touch target, contradicting the canonical --memox-touch-min: 48px design token
findings.evidenceA: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/tokens/spacing.css:47 "--memox-touch-min: 48px;" — the single canonical touch-target token used throughout the component source (e.g. flashcard-editor/components/Field.jsx:42 `minHeight: 'var(--memox-touch-min)'`).
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/flashcard-editor.md:45, guess-mode.md:40, import.md:43, languages.md:41, match-mode.md:41, and player.md:40 all state "touch targets ≥ 44×44" in their Handoff notes, while flashcard-list.md:55, mode-picker.md:42, and recall-mode.md:49 (all three manually 'Rebaselined 2026-07-18') correctly state "touch targets ≥ 48×48".
findings.description: Six of the ten specs assigned to this group carry a stale 44×44pt (iOS HIG) touch-target minimum in their generated Handoff notes, smaller than and inconsistent with the app's actual --memox-touch-min token (48px). This is systemic to the autogenerated-spec template (21 of 29 specs kit-wide use the wrong number) versus the manually rebaselined specs which state the correct value.
findings.impact: An engineer or QA reviewer using these specs as the acceptance bar for tap-target sizing would validate against a smaller (44px) minimum than the design system actually requires (48px), risking WCAG 2.5.5-adjacent accessibility shortfalls being signed off as compliant.
findings.recommendation: Fix the tool/ui_kit_shots/registry.mjs generator template (or its Handoff-notes constant) to read 48×48 from --memox-touch-min instead of a hard-coded 44×44, then regenerate all AUTOGENERATED specs (npm run gen:ui-kit).

findings.title: flashcard-list.md's 'Active state matrix' is missing 4 states that FlashcardList.jsx actually implements (selection-all, selection-empty, bulk-delete-confirm, bulk-outcome)
findings.evidenceA: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/flashcard-list.md:27-46 — the 16-row 'Active state matrix' table enumerates loaded/dense/minimum-data/long-text/empty/search/no-results/filter-applied/selection/add-sheet/card-actions/delete-confirm/loading/offline/error/not-found, and only references the deprecated convert-* trio outside the table.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/flashcard-list/FlashcardList.jsx implements `selection-all` (lines 159-165), `selection-empty` (lines 168-176), `bulk-delete-confirm` (lines 179-198), and `bulk-outcome` (lines 200-212) with substantial KIT-27-0x-referenced behavior — none of these 4 states appear anywhere in the spec's state matrix.
findings.description: This spec is explicitly 'manually maintained' (not autogenerated) per its own header note, so the omission is a genuine documentation gap rather than a stale codegen artifact. The 4 missing states cover meaningfully distinct, accessibility-relevant behavior (tri-state select-all, disabled-at-zero bulk actions, named destructive-count confirm, partial-success retry messaging) with no reference-PNG/verify coverage since they're absent from the table.
findings.impact: QA/engineers relying on flashcard-list.md as the canonical list of states to build/verify will miss the bulk-selection sub-flows entirely, and there is no Tier-1 evidence requirement tied to them since they're undocumented.
findings.recommendation: Add the 4 missing states to flashcard-list.md's Active state matrix so they get the same generated PNG/verify coverage as the other 16.

findings.title: Mode Picker's 'not-enough' state disables all five modes, but the business eligibility rule it quotes is Guess-specific
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/study-session/start-study-session.md:64 validation table row: "Guess không có ít nhất 5 meaning khác nhau | Chặn Start; yêu cầu thêm Card hoặc chọn scope khác" — the insufficient-distinct-meanings rule is scoped to Guess only; study-mode/README.md:7 "Mode chỉ chạy khi scope đáp ứng minimum eligibility của mode" implies each mode has its own threshold.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/mode-picker/ModePicker.jsx:25,32-34 — the `notEnough` flag (driven by copy naming Guess specifically: "Guess practice needs at least 5 cards with distinct meanings.", line 25) is passed as `disabled={notEnough}` to every `ModeOption` in the `MODES.map(...)` loop, disabling Review/Match/Guess/Recall/Fill uniformly.
findings.description: The 'not-enough' screen's own copy names Guess specifically as the mode with the unmet threshold, yet the implementation disables all 5 mode rows including Review, Match, Recall, and Fill, which the copy gives no indication require 5 distinct meanings.
findings.impact: A user who can't run Guess for lack of distinct meanings would also be blocked from Review/Match/Recall/Fill even if those modes' own eligibility thresholds are met, with copy that doesn't explain why non-Guess modes are disabled.
findings.recommendation: Either scope the disabled state to the Guess row only, or, if the real product decision is 'insufficient distinct meanings disables the whole picker', update the copy to state that generally rather than naming only Guess.

findings.title: FlashcardEditor has no 'stale target' state, though business requires one when the target deck is deleted/converted mid-edit
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/flashcard/create-flashcard.md:91 (§8) "Target became Parent/deleted → keep draft, return target picker." and §9 State matrix (line 98) lists 'stale target' as a required create-flow state.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/flashcard-editor.md:27-40 — the 10-state matrix (create/edit/validation/duplicate/additional-translation/audio-generating/keyboard-open/submitting/submit-error/submit-success) has no stale-target/target-picker-return state, and FlashcardEditor.jsx has no corresponding branch.
findings.description: When the deck a card is being created into becomes ineligible (deleted, or converted from Empty/Leaf to Parent) while the editor is open, business requires the draft be preserved and the user routed back to a target picker. No such screen or transition exists in the flashcard-editor feature.
findings.impact: This is a real (if rare) concurrent-edit edge case; without a design reference, engineers have no guidance on the required 'keep draft, return to target picker' UX.
findings.recommendation: Add a stale-target state to flashcard-editor.md/FlashcardEditor.jsx, or, if intentionally deferred, note the gap explicitly in the spec rather than silently omitting it.

findings.title: Delete-card confirmation copy in the design doesn't match business's mandated wording or content, and omits active-session impact
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/flashcard/delete-flashcard.md §4 (lines 38-45): "Delete this card? / '<term>' and its learning progress will be permanently deleted. Completed session summaries will stay. / Keep card                              Delete card" — safe action labeled 'Keep card', destructive labeled 'Delete card'; §Impact decision table (lines 49-57) also requires the confirm to reflect active/paused-session intersections.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/flashcard-list/FlashcardList.jsx:272-279 — title "Delete this card?", body "The card "안녕하세요" will be removed from this deck. This can't be undone.", actions labeled generically 'Cancel' / 'Delete' (not 'Keep card' / 'Delete card'), with no mention of learning progress, retained session summaries, or active-session intersection; the bulk variant (lines 188-195) similarly omits session-impact notes.
findings.description: The design's delete-confirmation copy diverges from the business-specified wording in both button labels and body content — it talks about the card being 'removed from this deck' rather than the term-quoted 'permanently deleted' framing business specifies, and never mentions the progress-loss / session-summaries-retained distinction or active-session impact business treats as essential.
findings.impact: Users get less complete information than business intends before an irreversible action (loss of scheduling/learning progress specifically, not just the card), and the bulk-confirm case doesn't address active-session intersections business explicitly calls for.
findings.recommendation: Align delete-confirm copy (button labels and body text) with delete-flashcard.md's template, and add the active-session-intersection note to the bulk-confirm state if that scenario is in scope.

findings.title: Import preview/commit lacks the valid/invalid/skipped/duplicate count breakdown and hierarchy-preview capability business requires
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/content-transfer/preview-import.md:8 "Valid/invalid/skipped/duplicate candidates có counts riêng." and line 9 "Hierarchy preview không được làm phẳng âm thầm."; docs/business/content-transfer/commit-import.md:11 "Summary phản ánh created/merged/skipped/failed chính xác."
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/import/Import.jsx:104-119 — the preview/mapping states only ever render a flat 2-column `Table` with a single aggregate label ("PREVIEW · 124 CARDS"), and the 'done' state (lines 48-56) reports only one aggregate count ("Imported 124 cards") with no created/merged/skipped/failed breakdown or hierarchy/tree presentation.
findings.description: Same root cause as C7-005 (no per-item duplicate resolution) surfacing at the preview/commit steps: because the design's import flow doesn't model Skip/Merge granularity, it also can't surface the counts breakdown or hierarchy-vs-flat preview business requires. Whether hierarchy import is in scope for this static-kit flow specifically is unclear and should be cross-checked against the deck/content-transfer feature area outside this workstream.
findings.impact: Same practical effect as C7-005: without breakdown counts, users can't verify what actually happened after a commit with duplicates or errors, only a single number.
findings.recommendation: Resolve alongside C7-005 — once the duplicate-resolution granularity decision is made, extend the preview/done states to report the resulting created/merged/skipped/failed counts.

findings.title: Several _features/*.jsx header comments list fewer states than the component actually implements (stale as new states were added)
findings.evidenceA: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/guess-mode/GuessMode.jsx:1 "States: waiting · correct · wrong · complete" (4 listed) vs. the code's 5 states including `long-text` (line 52) and the guess-mode.md spec's 5-state matrix.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/import/Import.jsx:1 "States: source · mapping · preview · dup-warning · done" (5 listed) vs. the code's 8 states including `importing`, `import-error`, `parent-target` (lines 21,38,61); similarly Languages.jsx:1 lists 5 states (missing `scripts`, present at line 51) and Player.jsx:1 lists 4 states (missing `error`, present at line 16).
findings.description: Four of the ten feature-local JSX files in this group have a top-of-file 'States:' comment that undercounts the states the component actually renders, indicating the comment wasn't updated when later states were added. FlashcardEditor.jsx, MatchMode.jsx, and RecallMode.jsx do not have this problem — their header comments are accurate.
findings.impact: Minor — a developer skimming only the header comment would under-estimate the number of states to implement/test, though the authoritative spec files are correct.
findings.recommendation: Update the four header comments (GuessMode.jsx, Import.jsx, Languages.jsx, Player.jsx) to list their full, current state sets.

findings.title: Recall's countdown badge has no data-* accessibility-intent annotation for the 10s/5s announcement thresholds, unlike the kit's established pattern
findings.evidenceA: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/recall-mode.md:43-44 (Timer behaviour) "Screen-reader status announces the remaining-time threshold and terminal timeout without announcing every tick."; docs/business/study-mode/recall-and-self-grade.md:72 "Screen reader không announce mỗi giây; announce các mốc 10 giây, 5 giây và 'Time's up'."
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/recall-mode/RecallMode.jsx:36-38 — the `MxBadge` timer element carries no `aria-live`, `role="timer"`, or `data-announce-*` annotation, unlike the kit's established convention for encoding runtime-only behavior as data-* attributes (compare Field.jsx:40 `data-validate-on="blur-then-submit"` or FlashcardEditor.jsx:144 `data-guard="dirty"`).
findings.description: The kit's convention elsewhere is to encode production-only interaction/accessibility intent that a static mock can't execute as explicit data-* attributes on the relevant node. The Recall timer's 10s/5s screen-reader announcement requirement is documented only in spec prose, not annotated on the MxBadge node itself.
findings.impact: Minor inconsistency in how implementation-facing intent is captured; slightly increases the chance the announcement-threshold detail gets missed relative to nodes that do carry the annotation.
findings.recommendation: Add a data-announce-thresholds="10,5" (or equivalent) annotation to the timer badge node, consistent with the rest of the kit's runtime-intent-via-data-attribute convention.

findings.title: GuessMode's 'complete' fixture uses done=20/total=20 progress with '5/5' correctness text — plausible but unlabeled, worth a comment
findings.evidenceA: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/guess-mode/GuessMode.jsx:35-38 — `<window.ProgressHeader done={20} total={20} .../>` alongside body text "You answered 5/5 correctly."
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/match-mode/MatchMode.jsx:34-37 has the identical pattern (`done={20} total={20}` with "You matched 5/5 pairs").
findings.description: The '20' progress figure and the '5/5' completion text refer to different things (likely: 20 = total cards in the stage across all mastery rounds, 5 = pairs/questions in the just-finished round) but nothing in the source comments this distinction, unlike most other numeric choices in this kit which are explained inline.
findings.impact: Purely a documentation-clarity issue; a future maintainer changing one number without understanding the other could introduce a real inconsistency.
findings.recommendation: Add a short inline comment clarifying that the ProgressHeader total tracks the whole stage while the completion text reports the just-finished round's count.

findings.title: Guard's active scope config still targets retired 'folders' path for library_overview_viewmodel.dart, contradicting the doc/WBS claim that this drift was already resolved
findings.evidenceA: tools/code-verification-guard/scopes/memox-scopes.yaml lines 198-208 (scope `presentation_async_action_controller_files`): includes `lib/presentation/features/folders/viewmodels/folder_detail_viewmodel.dart` and `lib/presentation/features/folders/viewmodels/library_overview_viewmodel.dart` — both under a `folders/` directory segment.
findings.evidenceB: docs/architecture/guard-compatibility.md lines 9-11 ('Resolved drift ... Retired Folder/Subfolder domain targets and examples use the canonical Deck/Library paths and vocabulary.') and docs/wbs/memox-v6-development-wbs.md line 245 ('0.4 CP | Align guard upstream | ... | Old `folders` paths removed/replaced with Deck paths ...'). The Lead's independently-verified list of the 27 canonical missing_target_path source paths uses `lib/presentation/features/library/viewmodels/library_overview_viewmodel.dart` (feature segment `library`, not `folders`) with no `folder_detail_viewmodel.dart` entry at all.
findings.description: The active `memox` ruleset (selected via tools/code-verification-guard/guard-manifest.yaml -> scopes: flutter-scopes.yaml + memox-scopes.yaml) still has a scope glob pointing at `lib/presentation/features/folders/viewmodels/...` for the async-action-controller pattern rule. This is documentation drift in the sense that two separate documents (an 'Accepted'/'Resolved' architecture doc dated 2026-07-18, and a WBS critical-path item) both assert this exact cleanup — removing stale `folders` paths in favor of Deck/Library paths — is already done, but the vendored guard config that ships in this repo has not actually been updated to match. This is not a business-vs-design conflict (rule 7 N/A); it is a case where the config lags what two governance docs already claim as complete.
findings.impact: Once WBS foundation work creates the real canonical file at `lib/presentation/features/library/viewmodels/library_overview_viewmodel.dart`, the `presentation_async_action_controller_files` scope will not match it (its include glob still points at a `folders/` directory that no longer exists in the approved layout), so the async-action-controller pattern rule will silently never scan that file. The rule intended to enforce a domain-critical convention for this async controller becomes a no-op for exactly the file it was meant to cover, and CI will show green with no warning that coverage is missing. This also directly undermines confidence in the 'Resolved drift' status the architecture doc claims and in WBS 0.4's critical-path completion claim — a live instance of the pattern the Lead warned about (a prior audit report claiming things are 'Closed' when the underlying artifact says otherwise).
findings.recommendation: Either (a) update `presentation_async_action_controller_files` in tools/code-verification-guard/scopes/memox-scopes.yaml to point at `lib/presentation/features/library/viewmodels/library_overview_viewmodel.dart` (and remove or re-home `folder_detail_viewmodel.dart` if that concept is genuinely retired), committing the change in the guard's own repo per its submodule contract, or (b) if `folders`/`folder_detail_viewmodel.dart` is intentionally still live for some reason, correct docs/architecture/guard-compatibility.md's 'Resolved drift' claim and WBS item 0.4's acceptance criteria to stop asserting the cleanup is complete. Then re-run the guard and confirm the scope now matches the intended canonical path.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: The `presentation_async_action_controller_files` scope glob in memox-scopes.yaml matches the actual canonical `library` feature path (or the doc/WBS claims are corrected to reflect the true state), and no scope in the active memox ruleset references a `folders/` segment for a concept the business/architecture docs treat as retired.

findings.title: No human-readable explanation exists for what memox.design_system.*, memox.routing.*, memox.study.*, or memox.performance.* rules require — only memox.hooks.* has prose documentation
findings.evidenceA: tools/code-verification-guard/README.md lines 176-190 ('MemoX Shared Hooks' section) and tools/code-verification-guard/docs/agent-yaml-contract.md lines 85-96 both give a plain-language explanation of what the hooks rules require (useMxSearchController, useMxTextValue/useMxTextSubmitState, useMxRequestFocusAfterFrame, `useMx...` prefix). No equivalent prose section exists anywhere in these files, in docs/code-verification-guard.md, or in any other doc/agent-*.md file for the design-system, routing, study, or performance rule families.
findings.evidenceB: docs/code-verification-guard.md (whole file) only documents the submodule setup, invocation, profile table, and a short 'MemoX v6 compatibility baseline' bullet list (namespace, SRS policy file location, WBS 1.8 gate) — it never explains rule intent for `memox.design_system.*`, `memox.routing.*`, `memox.study.*`, or `memox.performance.*` categories, all of which are registered in tools/code-verification-guard/guard-manifest.yaml lines 56-83 (e.g. `memox-design-system-rules.yaml`, `memox-routing-rules.yaml`, `memox-study-rules.yaml`, `memox-performance-rules.yaml`).
findings.description: Outside of the hooks rule family, the guard's rule intent for design-system, routing, study, and performance conventions exists only inside the raw YAML rule registries (per-rule `message`/`fix.hint` strings), which are not part of the documented entry points (docs/code-verification-guard.md, tools/code-verification-guard/README.md, or the guard's docs/*.md). There is no category-level prose overview anywhere in the assigned documentation set explaining, for example, what a design-token violation looks like, what routing rules forbid, or what study/performance rules check for.
findings.impact: A developer or reviewer who only reads the docs assigned to this workstream (the intended discovery path per docs/code-verification-guard.md's own pointers) has no way to learn what these four rule families actually enforce short of opening the raw rule-registry YAML files directly, which are outside the documented entry points and contain only per-rule strings, no category-level overview. This is especially costly for `memox.study.*`, since study/session/SRS logic is called out elsewhere (docs/code-verification-guard.md's compatibility baseline, ADR-003) as domain-critical.
findings.recommendation: Add a short prose subsection per rule category (design-system, routing, study, performance) to docs/code-verification-guard.md or a linked doc, mirroring the existing 'MemoX Shared Hooks' treatment — a sentence or two on what pattern each family targets and why, so the YAML is not the only source of truth for intent.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: docs/code-verification-guard.md or a linked doc contains a short human-readable summary of what memox.design_system.*, memox.routing.*, memox.study.*, and memox.performance.* rules check, comparable in depth to the existing hooks explanation.

findings.title: Legacy vendored rulesets (memox-v4, memox-v5, memox-design-jsx) sit undocumented alongside the active 'memox' ruleset inside the submodule
findings.evidenceA: Glob over tools/code-verification-guard/registries/projects/** shows four project rulesets: `memox`, `memox-v4`, `memox-v5`, `memox-design-jsx`, each with their own guard-manifest.yaml, config/, and rules/ folders.
findings.evidenceB: docs/code-verification-guard.md and tools/code-verification-guard/README.md/AGENTS.md only ever reference `--ruleset memox` and never mention or disambiguate memox-v4/memox-v5/memox-design-jsx; the root tools/code-verification-guard/guard-manifest.yaml `rule_sets.memox` entry (lines 56-83) is the only one wired in, so which of the four project folders is 'live' is only inferable by reading the manifest, not stated in any doc.
findings.description: Not a functional break (the active ruleset selection is unambiguous once you read guard-manifest.yaml), but a reader who browses the submodule directly, or greps for 'memox' rules, will find three other apparently-parallel MemoX rulesets with no note on whether they are historical snapshots, staging grounds for a future migration, or dead weight that should be pruned.
findings.impact: Wastes reviewer/maintainer time and creates risk that someone edits the wrong ruleset (e.g. memox-v5) believing it is active, or that stale rules from an older increment are mistaken for current policy when auditing.
findings.recommendation: Add a one-line note in tools/code-verification-guard/README.md or docs/code-verification-guard.md stating that memox-v4/memox-v5/memox-design-jsx are retired/historical snapshots (or explaining their real purpose), and consider pruning them from the vendored submodule if genuinely dead.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: A doc states the status/purpose of memox-v4, memox-v5, and memox-design-jsx rulesets relative to the active 'memox' ruleset.

findings.title: ci profile's per-rule severity override for python.no_print_in_src is redundant and undocumented
findings.evidenceA: tools/code-verification-guard/profiles/ci.yaml lines 6-16: `failure.warning_as_error: true` already promotes every warning to a failure under the ci profile, yet `overrides.severity.python.no_print_in_src: error` additionally hardcodes that one specific rule to `error` severity.
findings.evidenceB: docs/code-verification-guard.md lines 48-55 (profile table) describes ci only as 'treats warnings as errors too (stricter)' and does not mention or explain the python.no_print_in_src override, which is a no-op given warning_as_error is already true for the whole profile.
findings.description: Since `warning_as_error: true` already fails the ci run on any warning (including python.no_print_in_src if it were left at its default warning severity), the explicit severity override adds no behavior change but does add a maintenance question for anyone reading profiles/ci.yaml without context.
findings.impact: Purely cosmetic/maintenance confusion; no functional effect since the blanket warning_as_error setting already covers this rule.
findings.recommendation: Either remove the redundant override or add a one-line comment/doc note explaining why python.no_print_in_src is called out specifically (e.g. if it's meant to survive even if warning_as_error is ever turned off for ci).
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Either the redundant override is removed, or a comment/doc explains its purpose despite warning_as_error already covering it.

findings.title: pubspec.yaml declares neither Drift nor go_router despite ADRs treating both as Accepted architecture
findings.evidenceA: pubspec.yaml:30-59 (dependencies: flutter, cupertino_icons, flutter_riverpod ^3.2.1, riverpod_annotation 4.0.2, flutter_localizations, intl; dev_dependencies: flutter_test, flutter_lints ^6.0.0, build_runner ^2.15.1, riverpod_generator ^4.0.3 — no drift, sqlite3, drift_dev, path_provider, uuid, or go_router anywhere in the file)
findings.description: ADR-004 (lines 8-10) states 'Drift is the local canonical store... Each platform uses a tested opener,' docs/database/README.md line 9 states 'Drift is the local source of truth,' and ADR-006 (lines 8-11) states 'Use GoRouter with RouteNames, RoutePaths, and application navigation extensions/wrappers.' All three are listed as 'Accepted' in docs/architecture/README.md's decision table. Yet pubspec.yaml — the actual dependency manifest — declares none of drift, sqlite3, drift_dev, go_router, path_provider, or uuid. guard-compatibility.md (lines 20-34) frames the entire 'activation boundary' and WBS 1.8 remediation plan purely in terms of missing canonical source file paths ('Creating each approved canonical source path through its owning foundation item'); it never mentions pubspec dependency declarations. So there is no owner, WBS item, or tracked exception anywhere in this file set for the fact that the accepted tech stack isn't yet declared as a dependency.
findings.impact: A developer picking up WBS 1.8 work has no documented signal that pubspec.yaml itself is part of the activation debt; they could reasonably believe (per guard-compatibility.md's framing) that only the 27 canonical source files are outstanding. This risks either a stalled build (missing packages when someone starts writing lib/data/repositories/deck_repository_impl.dart or lib/app/router/app_router.dart) or an undocumented, unreviewed ad-hoc dependency choice that never goes through the same 'Accepted' ADR-quality scrutiny as the rest of the stack.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit pubspec-dependency checklist item to guard-compatibility.md's WBS 1.8 steps (or a new WBS item) naming the exact packages/version lines each ADR requires (drift + companions for ADR-004, go_router for ADR-006), so dependency-declaration debt is tracked with the same rigor as source-path debt.

findings.title: No ADR specifies reactive (Stream/watch) vs pull (Future/invalidate) query refresh semantics after mutations
findings.evidenceA: ADR-007-riverpod-and-testing.md:9-19 — 'UI watches state while callbacks use ref.read... Async state uses the shared MemoX loading/data/error/retry contract. Cancellation/disposal uses ref.onDispose and post-await liveness uses ref.mounted.' No mention of whether repository/query providers are backed by Drift's reactive `.watch()` streams (auto-refreshing StreamProviders) or one-shot Future queries requiring an explicit ref.invalidate/ref.invalidateSelf after a command.
findings.description: docs/database/schema-v1.md's 'Atomic operations' section (lines 51-64) describes several state-changing transactions (e.g. 'Apply one terminal aggregate outcome + Progress schedule exactly once,' 'Finalize session + contribution events exactly once') that must be reflected in Due queue, session, and progress UI immediately afterward, but neither ADR-007 nor the database docs say whether that reflection happens automatically via a Drift reactive stream or must be triggered by an explicit provider invalidation call after the command succeeds.
findings.impact: Without this decision, two engineers can implement the same 'commit terminal answer -> due queue updates' flow with incompatible patterns (one stream-based, one invalidate-based), producing stale-UI bugs (a card answered correctly still shown as due) or redundant re-fetch churn that the p95 budgets in tier1-resource-budgets.md (e.g. 'Due queue page 150ms/350ms p95') don't budget for.
findings.recommendation: Add a short paragraph to ADR-007 (or a new architecture note) stating the canonical refresh mechanism for query providers after a repository mutation — e.g. 'query providers wrap Drift .watch() streams; commands never call ref.invalidate directly' or the reverse — so the guard and future WBS items can hold implementations to one pattern.

findings.title: Guard's canonical hooks file implies Flutter Hooks usage, but no ADR documents this and pubspec has no flutter_hooks/hooks_riverpod dependency
findings.evidenceA: ADR-007-riverpod-and-testing.md (full file) — describes only Riverpod Annotation/codegen provider patterns and never mentions Flutter Hooks, hooks_riverpod, or any '_hooks.dart' naming convention; the file lib/presentation/shared/hooks/mx_search_controller_hooks.dart is one of the guard's 27 canonical paths per the confirmed ground truth, and pubspec.yaml (lines 30-59) declares no flutter_hooks or hooks_riverpod package.
findings.description: The canonical path name 'mx_search_controller_hooks.dart' under a directory literally named 'hooks' strongly suggests the Flutter Hooks package convention (a widget-lifecycle hook, e.g. useSearchController-style), but the only ADR governing state-management patterns (ADR-007) is silent on hooks entirely and describes a pure Riverpod-Annotation approach without hooks.
findings.impact: Without clarification, an implementer may either (a) add flutter_hooks/hooks_riverpod as an undocumented, un-reviewed architectural addition, or (b) misname a non-hooks helper class '..._hooks.dart', causing confusion about whether the project uses two competing state-composition patterns (plain Riverpod codegen vs hooks).
findings.recommendation: Either add a sentence to ADR-007 sanctioning flutter_hooks/hooks_riverpod for specific widget-local composition (matching the guard's canonical path), or rename the guard's canonical path/target if 'hooks' here is not meant to reference the Flutter Hooks package.

findings.title: Default Flutter counter-app placeholder content (main.dart, widget_test.dart, ARB files) has no documented replacement tracking
findings.evidenceA: lib/main.dart:1-64 (MemoxApp/HomePage counter demo with pushCounterMessage/incrementTooltip), test/widget_test.dart:14-29 ('Counter increments smoke test'), lib/l10n/app_en.arb:1-19 and lib/l10n/app_vi.arb:1-8 (appTitle/homeTitle/pushCounterMessage/incrementTooltip — stock `flutter create` template strings, no MemoX domain vocabulary such as Deck/Flashcard/SRS)
findings.description: guard-compatibility.md's activation-debt tracking (WBS 1.8, lines 20-34) is scoped only to the 27 canonical paths that don't yet exist; it says nothing about the files that already exist (lib/main.dart, test/widget_test.dart, the ARB files) and are 100% unmodified `flutter create` boilerplate unrelated to MemoX's domain. ADR-005 (line 9) says 'main.dart delegates to a small bootstrap,' which main.dart does not currently do.
findings.impact: A reviewer relying solely on guard-compatibility.md's framing of 'what's outstanding' would not learn that main.dart/widget_test.dart/ARB content itself needs to be replaced (not just supplemented) once lib/app/bootstrap/app_bootstrap.dart and friends exist; this is low risk but is currently untracked by any doc in this file set.
findings.recommendation: Add one line to guard-compatibility.md or WBS 1.8 noting that lib/main.dart, test/widget_test.dart, and the ARB files are template placeholders slated for replacement (not extension) once the bootstrap/router foundation lands.

findings.title: WBS 'CP' critical-path marking is inconsistent with the document's own dependency graph
findings.evidenceA: docs/wbs/memox-v6-development-wbs.md:410 — `5.7.5 CP | First-learning release gate | L | 0.5, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8, 3.12, 4.10, 5.7.4 | ...` (1.8 listed as an explicit, non-range dependency of the final release gate). Also line 318: `4.10 CP | ... | 0.5, 1.2–1.6, 1.8–1.11, 2.10, 4.1–4.8 | ...` (range includes 1.8). Also line 261: `1.9 CP | Localization foundation | M | 1.1, 0.6 | ...` (0.6 listed directly). Also line 262: `1.10 CP | Shared test infrastructure | L | 1.1–1.7 | ...` (range includes 1.7). Also line 278: `2.10 CP | Foundation contract tests | L | 2.2–2.9 | ...` (range includes 2.9). Also lines 297 and 299: `3.10 CP | ... | 3.2–3.9 | ...` and `3.12 CP | ... | 3.1–3.10, 4.10 | ...` (both ranges include 3.8).
findings.evidenceB: The rows for the dependencies themselves carry no CP marker: line 247 `| 0.6 | Define release scope/exceptions | S | 0.2 | ...` (no CP), line 259 `| 1.7 | Developer fixtures | M | 1.3 | ...` (no CP), line 260 `| 1.8 | Raise guard profile | S | 1.1–1.7 | ...` (no CP), line 277 `| 2.9 | High-contrast readiness | M | 2.7, 0.6 | ...` (no CP), line 295 `| 3.8 | Selection/control primitives | L | 3.1–3.4 | ...` (no CP). The document defines CP as marking 'the critical path to the first complete learning release' (line 236), and docs/audits/documentation-remediation-2026-07-18.md:45 claims the WBS has a 'corrected critical path' and line 88 claims 'First-learning critical path is explicit and acyclic'.
findings.description: Independently expanding every dependency cell in the WBS (including ranges like '1.1–1.7' and '2.2–2.9') shows that five items — 0.6, 1.7, 1.8, 2.9, 3.8 — are direct or range-based prerequisites of items that ARE marked CP, up to and including the terminal release gate 5.7.5 itself (which lists 1.8 as an explicit dependency, not even via a range). Yet none of these five items carry the CP marker. This is either (a) a labeling omission — these items really are on the critical path and should be flagged CP — or (b) the dependency ranges are looser than they read (e.g. '1.1–1.7' was meant to exclude 1.7) and should be tightened to an explicit list. As written, a reader following the CP column to find the minimum blocking chain to the first-learning release would miss five real prerequisites, several of which (1.8 'Raise guard profile', 1.7 'Developer fixtures') gate the guard-clean state required by DoD item 12 ('the consolidated repository verifier passes') for the release gate itself.
findings.impact: Anyone using the CP column to sequence/staff the first-learning release (as the WBS explicitly instructs: 'Release the first-learning journey after WBS 5.7.5', §11) will under-scope the critical path and may deprioritize 0.6/1.7/1.8/2.9/3.8, causing late discovery that 5.7.5 (which directly depends on 1.8) or 4.10/1.9/2.10/3.10/3.12 (which depend on the others) cannot close. It also directly contradicts the prior audit's claim (line 45, line 88) that the critical path has already been 'corrected' and is 'explicit'.
findings.recommendation: Either add the CP marker to 0.6, 1.7, 1.8, 2.9 and 3.8, or narrow the dependency ranges on 1.9, 1.10, 2.10, 3.10, 3.12, 4.10 and 5.7.5 so they no longer literally include these items, and re-run the dependency-graph trace to confirm the CP set is closed under 'depends-on'.

findings.title: Presentation-layer folder contract forbids a 'viewmodels' folder under feature roots, but the guard's canonical paths require exactly that, for three critical-path features
findings.evidenceA: docs/wbs/memox-v6-development-wbs.md:101 (Contract thư mục table): `| lib/presentation/features/<feature>/ | Feature presentation | screens, widgets, providers, optional routes | data imports, raw DB/network/JSON/IO |` — only screens/widgets/providers/routes are Allowed; 'viewmodels' is not listed. Confirmed by the proposed tree at line 147: `└─ features/<feature>/{screens,widgets,providers,routes}/`. §5.1 (lines 155-167) further specifies that presentation/mutable state should be 'generated class-based Notifier/AsyncNotifier' providers exposing methods like `createDeck`, `saveCard` — i.e. a Riverpod-provider pattern, not a separate ViewModel class layer.
findings.evidenceB: Lead-confirmed guard canonical paths (ground truth, not from a doc I can cite by line, but authoritative per task framing) include: lib/presentation/features/decks/viewmodels/deck_detail_viewmodel.dart, lib/presentation/features/flashcards/viewmodels/flashcard_editor_viewmodel.dart, lib/presentation/features/flashcards/viewmodels/flashcard_list_viewmodel.dart, lib/presentation/features/library/viewmodels/library_overview_viewmodel.dart — all four require a `viewmodels/` subfolder directly under a feature root, which the folder contract in evidenceA disallows. (Note: the WBS tree does allow a `viewmodels/` bucket, but only under `lib/presentation/shared/` — line 146 `shared/{widgets,layouts,dialogs,bottom_sheets,feedback,hooks,viewmodels}/` — not under `features/<feature>/`.)
findings.description: This is not a Business-vs-Design conflict (so not forced to P0 under rule 7) but it is a direct, evidenced conflict between the WBS's own normative architecture contract (§4.2, explicitly framed as Allowed/Forbidden, not illustrative) and the actual guard configuration that will gate every PR. Three of the four affected files map to WBS critical-path items: deck_detail_screen/viewmodel → 5.2.4 CP 'Library and open Deck', flashcard_editor_viewmodel → 5.3.2 CP 'Card Editor', flashcard_list_viewmodel → 5.3.3 CP 'Flashcard list/minimum management', library_overview_viewmodel → 5.2.4 CP. Implementers following §4.2/§4.3 literally will place these files under `providers/`, which the guard will then flag; implementers following the guard will create a `viewmodels/` folder the WBS explicitly forbids and will use a ViewModel-class pattern the WBS's Riverpod contract (§5.1) does not describe.
findings.impact: Blocks or destabilizes FD-08/FD-09 (Wire DI / Implement presentation state) for at least three critical-path work packages (5.2.4, 5.3.2, 5.3.3) — implementers cannot satisfy both the documented folder contract and the guard simultaneously without an explicit reconciling decision.
findings.recommendation: Architecture/Guard owners (per WBS 0.4 'Align guard upstream', which is exactly the mechanism named for this class of problem) must decide and record whether the presentation-state layer is a Riverpod-generated provider/notifier (per §5.1) named and located as `providers/*_provider.dart` — in which case the guard config's four `viewmodels/*.dart` canonical paths are stale and should be corrected — or whether a distinct `viewmodels/` folder is an accepted addition to the §4.2 Allowed list for feature roots, in which case §4.2 and §4.3 should be updated to say so.

findings.title: No decision table exists for Recall mode's 20-second timer race / pause-resume behavior, even though the WBS calls it out as a required test target
findings.evidenceA: docs/wbs/memox-v6-development-wbs.md:381 — `5.5.6 CP | Mode contract tests | XL | 5.5.5 | Shared contract tests over all modes plus Guess five-options, Recall 20s race, Fill normalization and Match classification.` and line 400 `5.6.14 CP | Study test pyramid | ... durable-answer, Recall 20s and exactly-once terminal scheduling rows all have test IDs.` Both explicitly expect stable, row-referenced test coverage for 'Recall 20s race' analogous to Fill/Match.
findings.evidenceB: docs/decision-tables/README.md:6-12 catalogs exactly six contracts (ST-SESSION-TYPE-v1, ST-CONTENT-CHANGE-v1, SM-MATCH-v1, SM-FILL-v1, SRS8-v1, DATA-MERGE-v1); a directory Glob of docs/decision-tables confirms no seventh file exists for Recall (or Review/Guess) outcome/timing classification. SM-MATCH-v1 (match-outcomes.md) and SM-FILL-v1 (fill-answer-normalization.md) exist for their respective modes with explicit row IDs referenced by 'row-to-test parity' (WBS FD-14, line 226), but Recall's deadline-vs-tap race, pause/resume time restoration (also required by WBS 5.6.12 line 398: 'Recall remaining time/order/failed set restored') and reveal/remembered/forgot branching have no equivalent stable-ID table.
findings.description: Work-item-schema.md states 'A branch-heavy business item also links its stable decision-table row IDs and row-to-test mappings' (line 31-32). Recall's timer race and pause/resume restoration is exactly this kind of branch-heavy, concurrency-sensitive behavior (comparable in shape to SM-MATCH's race/duplicate-tap rows SM-MATCH-005/006 or SM-FILL's retry row SM-FILL-007), yet unlike Match and Fill it has no dedicated table to anchor stable row IDs for tests.
findings.impact: Without a stable-ID table, 'Recall 20s race' tests referenced by WBS 5.5.6/5.6.14 have no canonical row set to satisfy the decision-tables/README's coverage-checker contract ('Coverage checker fail khi row ID không có test hoặc test dẫn ID không tồn tại'), risking ad hoc, non-traceable test coverage for a timing-sensitive first-learning-critical-path feature.
findings.recommendation: Add an SM-RECALL-v1 (or similarly named) decision table enumerating Recall's reveal/remembered/forgot/deadline-tap-race/pause-resume rows, mirroring the structure of match-outcomes.md and fill-answer-normalization.md, and reference it from decision-tables/README.md.

findings.title: The 'decision_gate' field required by work-item-schema.md has no explicit resolution mechanism in the register, especially for sections 6–16
findings.evidenceA: docs/traceability/work-item-schema.md:21 — `| decision_gate | Accepted ADR/decision IDs or none |` is listed as a field every WBS work package must resolve 'from its row plus the register' (line 6-7).
findings.evidenceB: docs/traceability/work-item-register.md:9-35 (Prefix defaults table) has columns Prefix | Owner | Domain | Canonical inputs | Default guard/test — there is no 'decision_gate' column at all, for any prefix. The only decision-gate data in the file is the 'Accepted decision gates' table (lines 39-46, DG-01..DG-06, which only maps to §0.x/architecture-wide ADRs) and the 'First-learning trace' table (lines 50-60), which only covers WBS ranges up to 5.7.5 and mixes ADR references into an 'Architecture/data' column rather than a distinct decision_gate field. Sections 6.* through 16.* (docs/wbs/memox-v6-development-wbs.md lines 412-526) — more than half the WBS's 151 items — have no decision_gate value anywhere, even though some of those items are explicitly conditional/approval-gated in their own text: line 501 `14.5 | Account deletion conditional flow | L | 14.2 | Only if product support is approved; ...` and line 511 `15.4 | Cloud backup conditional flow | XL | 14.1, 15.3 | Only after provider/retention/security approval.`
findings.description: 14.5 and 15.4 describe exactly the kind of product-approval gate that DG-01..DG-06 formalize (owner, ADR, status, date) for the foundation decisions, but they have no tracked gate ID, owner, or acceptance date — the approval is just prose ('Only if...', 'Only after...') with no register entry to check against. For the rest of sections 6–16, decision_gate presumably defaults to 'none' (a valid schema value), but this is never stated, so it is not possible to tell from the docs whether 'none' was a deliberate resolution or simply an unaddressed field.
findings.impact: Reduces auditability of exactly the conditional/high-risk items (account deletion, cloud backup) that most need a recorded approval gate; a reviewer cannot distinguish 'decision_gate intentionally none' from 'decision_gate never resolved' for roughly 60 WBS items.
findings.recommendation: Either add a decision_gate column to the Prefix defaults table (defaulting explicitly to 'none' where applicable), or add DG-07/DG-08-style tracked gates with owner/status/date for the 14.5 and 15.4 conditional flows, consistent with how DG-01..DG-06 are tracked for §0.x.

findings.title: Proposed source tree (§4.3) omits several paths that are named as canonical elsewhere in the same document and confirmed by the guard config
findings.evidenceA: docs/wbs/memox-v6-development-wbs.md:132-138 (domain tree): `domain/ ├─ entities/ ├─ value_objects/ ├─ repositories/ ├─ usecases/<business_object>/ ├─ policies/ └─ study_modes/` — there is no `learning_progress/` subfolder. Also lines 117-122 (router tree): `router/ ├─ app_router.dart ├─ app_navigation.dart ├─ route_names.dart ├─ route_paths.dart └─ registries/` — no `redirect.dart` or `route_placeholder.dart` listed.
findings.evidenceB: The same document repeatedly and explicitly names `lib/domain/learning_progress/srs_8_box_policy.dart` as the sole canonical SRS path: line 46 (DG-04) `SRS transition math has one source at lib/domain/learning_progress/srs_8_box_policy.dart` and line 368 (5.4.3 CP) `The sole pure-domain implementation is lib/domain/learning_progress/srs_8_box_policy.dart`. The Lead's confirmed guard canonical-path list also includes lib/app/router/redirect.dart and lib/app/router/route_placeholder.dart, neither of which appears in the §4.3 tree.
findings.description: §4.3 is explicitly labeled 'Cây source đề xuất' (proposed source tree), so this is lower-severity than a normative contract violation, and the canonical SRS path is stated correctly and prominently three times elsewhere, so an implementer is unlikely to be misled. Still, an illustrative tree that omits the one folder (`learning_progress/`) housing the single most architecturally load-bearing file in the whole WBS (DG-04's frozen SRS policy path) and two guard-required router files is a completeness gap worth closing.
findings.impact: Low — mainly a clarity/consistency issue for anyone scaffolding the tree literally from §4.3 rather than cross-referencing DG-04/5.4.3.
findings.recommendation: Add `learning_progress/` under the domain tree and `redirect.dart`/`route_placeholder.dart` under the router tree in §4.3 so the illustrative tree matches the guard's 27 canonical paths exactly.

findings.title: Two decision-table row-ID prefixes don't match their catalog family name
findings.evidenceA: docs/decision-tables/README.md:7-8 — `| [ST-SESSION-TYPE-v1](./study-session-types.md) | ... |` and `| [ST-CONTENT-CHANGE-v1](./study-session-content-changes.md) | ... |`
findings.evidenceB: docs/decision-tables/study-session-types.md:5-16 rows are all IDed `ST-TYPE-001`..`ST-TYPE-012` (not `ST-SESSION-TYPE-NNN`); docs/decision-tables/study-session-content-changes.md:6-16 rows are all IDed `ST-CHG-001`..`ST-CHG-010` (not `ST-CONTENT-CHANGE-NNN`). By contrast SM-MATCH-v1/SM-FILL-v1/SRS8-v1/DATA-MERGE-v1 row IDs match their family name exactly.
findings.description: Not a uniqueness problem (Lead already confirmed 0 duplicate IDs) and not functionally broken, but it is an inconsistent convention within the same catalog — 4 of 6 families use the full family name as the row prefix, 2 use an abbreviation, which makes it slightly harder to grep/verify 'test dẫn ID không tồn tại' (per README's coverage-checker rule) by family name alone.
findings.impact: Cosmetic; may cause minor friction for a coverage-checker script or a new contributor searching by family name.
findings.recommendation: Either rename the two families in README.md to ST-TYPE-v1 / ST-CHG-v1 to match their row prefixes, or rename the row prefixes to ST-SESSION-TYPE-/ST-CONTENT-CHANGE- to match the catalog names.

findings.title: Decision-table column headers are inconsistent with the catalog's own stated Given/When/Then convention
