findings.description: The business Answer Study Stage contract makes the unlimited mastery-round loop (round-complete distinct from mode-complete, retry-round with the deduped failed set) a canonical state requirement for every graded mode, and additionally requires Guess to handle an invalid distractor pool. None of the three graded-mode design specs (Match, Guess, Recall) enumerate a round-complete or retry-round state, and Guess has no invalid-pool state. This is a direct Business-vs-Design disagreement on the set of screen states for the core answer flow; the Product Owner must decide whether the design state matrices are expanded to the business loop or the business loop is renegotiated.
findings.impact: The core learning loop (repeat failed cards until a clean round) has no design surface for its intermediate states. An engineer building from the design specs would ship a single-pass grade with a bare 'complete', silently dropping the mastery-round retry loop and Guess invalid-pool recovery — a core-flow implementability blocker.
findings.rootCause: Design mode-state matrices were authored around single-pass grade states and never reconciled with the business mastery-round loop invariant.
findings.recommendation: Product Owner decides the canonical state set; if business stands, add round-complete/retry-round states to match-mode/guess-mode/recall-mode specs and an invalid-distractor-pool state to guess-mode, with reference evidence. Do not resolve by silently trusting either side.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Each graded-mode spec's state matrix contains round-complete and retry-round states (and Guess an invalid-pool state), or a PO decision record supersedes answer-study-stage §10/§11.

findings.title: Graded-mode design specs omit the saving / answer-save-error / stale-conflict / missing-card recoverable states business mandates
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/study-session/answer-study-stage.md:110 (§10) — "Saving, answer-save-error, retry success, stale conflict, missing Card"; §9 L99-104 error copy table; §11 L123-124 stale writer / content mutation race acceptance.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/match-mode.md:27-36 and guess-mode.md:28-35 — state matrices contain no saving/answer-save-error/stale/missing-card state; component maps contain no recoverable-feedback/error surface.
findings.description: Business requires the answer flow to present Saving, answer-save-error (with the 'Your answer is still here' copy), stale-checkpoint, and missing-card states across the graded modes. The design mode specs' state matrices enumerate only interaction/outcome states and omit these recoverable states entirely. Even if the save/error surface is intended to live in the shared study shell (WBS 5.6.10), no design spec in the answer flow enumerates these states, so the durable-answer error UX has no design representation. Documentation gap, not a value conflict.
findings.impact: The save-failure/retry and stale-writer recovery UX — central to the durable-answer guarantee — has no design surface an engineer can build against, risking an answer flow with no recoverable error presentation.
findings.recommendation: Enumerate saving/answer-save-error/stale-conflict/missing-card states in the graded-mode specs or explicitly delegate them to a named shared study-shell spec and reference it from each mode spec.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Each graded-mode answer surface documents (or references) the saving, answer-save-error, stale-conflict, and missing-card states required by answer-study-stage §10.

findings.title: No decision table for Recall 20s timeout / tap-deadline race, unlike Match (SM-MATCH-v1) and Fill (SM-FILL-v1)
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/study-mode/map-mode-outcome.md:35-36 — "Recall timeout event chỉ hợp lệ với timer identity/version, threshold 20 giây... Manual reveal/self-grade và timeout dùng một resolution identity; event đến sau khi resolution đã commit trả prior result hoặc conflict"; docs/business/study-session/answer-study-stage.md:66 — "Recall tap/deadline race được serialize theo timer-resolution identity."
findings.evidenceB: docs/decision-tables/ contains match-outcomes.md (SM-MATCH-v1) and fill-answer-normalization.md (SM-FILL-v1) but no recall/timeout table; WBS 5.6.14 (line 400) requires "Recall 20s ... rows all have test IDs" and 5.5.6 (L381) "Recall 20s race".
findings.description: Match and Fill have executable decision tables with row IDs that satisfy the row-to-test parity contract; Recall's timeout classification and the tap-vs-timeout deadline-race serialization are specified only in prose across map-mode-outcome.md and answer-study-stage.md. The WBS explicitly calls out the Recall 20s race as a required test target and requires those rows to have test IDs, but no decision table exists to enumerate them. This matches the E-WBS reviewer's E-003 finding, confirmed here from the answer-flow side.
findings.impact: The most timing-sensitive, race-prone branch of the answer flow (timer expiry vs. user reveal/self-grade) has no enumerated Given/When/Then rows, so row-to-test parity cannot be established and edge cases (double-resolution, late timeout after commit) may go untested.
findings.recommendation: Author an ST-RECALL-v1 (or equivalent) decision table enumerating timeout-valid, timeout-invalid, tap-before-deadline, tap-after-deadline, and duplicate-resolution rows, and link it from map-mode-outcome.md/answer-study-stage.md as Match/Fill do.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: A Recall timeout/race decision table exists with row IDs referenced by answer-study-stage.md and map-mode-outcome.md and mapped to WBS 5.6.14 test IDs.

findings.title: match-mode and guess-mode handoff notes state a 44x44 minimum touch target, contradicting the canonical 48px touch-min token
findings.evidenceA: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/match-mode.md:41 — "touch targets ≥ 44×44"; guess-mode.md:41 — "touch targets ≥ 44×44".
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/recall-mode.md:49 and dashboard.md:51 — "touch targets ≥ 48×48"; canonical token --memox-touch-min: 48px (per SRS/C7 ground truth), and WBS FD-12 (line 224) mandates a "48px target baseline".
findings.description: Within the same answer flow, two graded-mode specs (Match, Guess) document a 44x44 minimum touch target while the sibling Recall spec, the Today spec, the WBS FD-12 baseline, and the design system's own --memox-touch-min: 48px token all say 48. Per AGENTS.md the design-kit token is the source of truth for visual values, so the 44x44 figure is documentation drift against the canonical token. This is the answer-flow slice of the systemic kit-wide 44-vs-48 defect (C7-009).
findings.impact: An engineer following match/guess handoff notes could build 44px hit targets, below the 48px accessibility baseline the token and WBS require, producing a real accessibility regression on the core answer screens.
findings.rootCause: Stale 44x44 figure carried over from the pre-Flutter 390x780 baseline into autogenerated handoff notes and not rebaselined against the 48px token.
findings.recommendation: Correct match-mode.md and guess-mode.md handoff notes to 48x48 to match the --memox-touch-min token and the sibling specs; audit the autogeneration source (registry) so it stops emitting 44x44.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: match-mode.md and guess-mode.md state touch targets >= 48x48 consistent with --memox-touch-min and recall-mode.md.

findings.title: 'Failed set' (intra-session mastery retry) and 'missed Card set' (inter-session Relearn) are used across the retry chain without an explicit equivalence/distinction statement
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/study-session/answer-study-stage.md:40,69 — retry round shuffles the "failed set" / `nextRoundFailedCardIds`; §1 L15 "Mastery retry round... khác với... một future Relearn session."
findings.evidenceB: docs/business/study-session/relearn-cards.md:5,13 and docs/business/learning-progress/surface-due-cards.md:22,39 — Relearn is built from "terminal wrong" / "missed terminal Card identities," a separate snapshot; no doc states how the intra-session failed set relates to the inter-session missed set.
findings.description: The Retry flow overloads two adjacent but distinct concepts: the intra-session mastery failed set (cards that must be re-shown within the same session before a mode completes) and the inter-session missed/relearn set (cards that got a terminal wrong and are eligible for a separate future Relearn session). Each doc uses its own term correctly, but no single sentence states their relationship (a card in the failed set is not yet a relearn item; it becomes a missed item only after the terminal outcome commits). This matches E-008 from the WBS reviewer, observed here in the retry chain.
findings.impact: A reader tracing 'retry' can conflate mastery retry-rounds with Relearn, risking an implementation that injects failed-set cards into a Relearn queue mid-session (which relearn-cards.md §8 L81 explicitly forbids) or reshuffles across the wrong boundary.
findings.recommendation: Add one explicit equivalence/distinction note (in answer-study-stage.md or study-session/README.md) defining failed set vs missed/relearn set and the terminal-commit boundary that converts one into eligibility for the other.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: A canonical doc states the relationship between the intra-session failed set and the inter-session missed/relearn set and the boundary between them.

findings.title: study-result design state matrix omits the 'No more eligible cards' result branch that finalize-study-session defines
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/study-session/finalize-study-session.md:85 (§7 result branches) — "No more eligible cards | Completion copy | Done" (a distinct result branch with its own completion copy and Done primary).
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/study-result.md:29-37 — 7 states: standard, goal-met, goal-missed, many-wrong, finalizing, retry-finalize, finalize-error; there is no no-more-eligible-cards / caught-up state.
findings.description: Business defines five result branches (Standard, Goal met, Goal missed, Many wrong, No more eligible cards). The design study-result spec renders four of them (standard/goal-met/goal-missed/many-wrong) plus three process states (finalizing/retry-finalize/finalize-error) but has no state for the 'no more eligible cards' completion branch, whose primary action is Done rather than Continue studying. Documentation gap between the business result-branch table and the design state matrix.
findings.impact: When a learner finishes the last eligible cards, the design has no state showing the completion copy and Done-primary layout the business branch specifies; the flow would fall back to the generic 'standard' state with the wrong primary CTA (Continue studying instead of Done).
findings.recommendation: Add a 'no-more-eligible' (caught-up) state to study-result.md mapping to finalize §7's fifth branch, with Done as primary; or record a decision to merge it into an existing state with correct CTA handling.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: study-result.md state matrix contains a state satisfying finalize-study-session.md:85 (completion copy, Done primary).

findings.title: Relearn session's 'cards to revisit' count header (entered from Study Result 'Review missed') has no dedicated design surface/spec
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/study-session/relearn-cards.md:44-49 (§4 composition) — "Relearn / <remaining count> cards to revisit / <prompt + interaction> / [ Next ]"; finalize-study-session.md:93 — Review missed starts a new relearn session.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/ — mode specs (match/guess/recall) reference window.ProgressHeader for stage progress but no spec documents a relearn-specific header or the 'N cards to revisit' remaining-count surface; there is no relearn screen spec.
findings.description: Finalize's 'Review missed' action starts a Relearn session, which business specifies with its own header composition ('Relearn / <remaining count> cards to revisit'). The design kit reuses the graded-mode screens for relearn but no spec documents where the relearn label and remaining-count live, so the relearn presentation entered from Study Result has no design representation of its distinguishing header.
findings.impact: Ambiguity for the engineer building the Review-missed handoff: whether relearn reuses the standard ProgressHeader or needs the distinct 'cards to revisit' header business shows; risk of shipping relearn indistinguishable from a normal session.
findings.recommendation: Document the relearn header/remaining-count surface (either a note in the mode specs' relearn use or a small relearn spec) mapping to relearn-cards.md §4.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: A design spec documents the relearn header and remaining-count surface matching relearn-cards.md:44-49.

findings.title: study-result.md cites an npm/Node autogeneration + screenshot pipeline (gen:ui-kit, tool/ui_kit_shots) that does not exist in this Flutter repository
findings.evidenceA: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/study-result.md:3-5 — "AUTOGENERATED from tool/ui_kit_shots/registry.mjs — do not hand-edit; run `npm run gen:ui-kit`... Verify: `node tool/ui_kit_shots/shoot.mjs study-result` (gated by `npm run verify:ui-kit`)."
findings.evidenceB: Repo is Flutter local-first (WBS L4 "ứng dụng Flutter local-first"); no package.json / tool/ui_kit_shots pipeline exists (consistent with C4-002 and C5-011 which verified these tool paths and shots PNGs are absent).
findings.description: The Study Result spec's provenance and verification instructions point to a Node.js UI-kit shot pipeline and npm scripts that do not exist in this repository, and to reference PNGs the audit found are not present. Documentation drift: an engineer told to regenerate or verify this spec via 'npm run gen:ui-kit' / 'node tool/ui_kit_shots/shoot.mjs' cannot do so. This is the completion-flow instance of the systemic C5-011 pipeline-drift finding.
findings.impact: The spec's 'source of truth' and 'verify' claims are unreproducible; the Study Result spec cannot be regenerated or its parity re-verified as documented, undermining trust in it as the completion-screen contract.
findings.rootCause: Specs carried over their React-Native/Node autogeneration boilerplate through the Flutter rebaseline without updating tooling references.
findings.recommendation: Replace the Node/npm autogeneration and shot-verification header with the actual Flutter regeneration/verification path (or mark it historical) so the completion-screen spec is reproducible.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: study-result.md provenance/verify instructions reference tooling that exists in this repo (or are marked non-operative).

findings.title: SRS8-v1 decision table omits the Box 7 → wrong transition despite being titled 'exhaustive'
findings.evidenceA: docs/decision-tables/srs-8-box-v1.md line 1 title 'Leitner 8 Box exhaustive decision table'; line 13 SRS8-007 'Box 7 | correct | Box 8 | null'. The wrong transition is enumerated for every other activated box — SRS8-004 (Box 1 wrong), SRS8-018 (Box 2 wrong), SRS8-020 (Box 3 wrong), SRS8-006 (Box 4 wrong), SRS8-022 (Box 5 wrong), SRS8-024 (Box 6 wrong), SRS8-009 (Box 8 wrong → Box 7, +120d) — but there is no 'Box 7 | wrong | Box 6 | nowUtc + 60d' row.
findings.description: The decision table that schedule-next-review.md names as its algorithm source of truth (schedule-next-review.md line 5) claims to be exhaustive and its own decision-tables/README convention requires row-to-test parity, yet the wrong-answer transition out of Box 7 is missing. The policy math (schedule-next-review.md §2: wrong → max(box-1,1); Box1..7 intervals 1/3/7/14/30/60/120) unambiguously implies Box 7 wrong → Box 6 with a +60d dueAt, so the correct value is derivable, but a test author generating cases one-row-per-transition from the 'exhaustive' table will silently skip this transition.
findings.impact: A high-box lapse (Box 7 → wrong) is the most consequential demotion in the ladder; leaving it out of the exhaustive table risks it being untested and mis-scheduled (e.g. wrong interval applied) with no failing row to catch it.
findings.rootCause: Table was hand-extended (rows 017-028) after the original 001-016 summary without a completeness check over all box×event pairs.
findings.recommendation: Add row 'Box 7 | wrong | Box 6 | nowUtc + 60d' to srs-8-box-v1.md and give it a stable SRS8-0xx ID, then reconcile the ID-range statement in the header.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: srs-8-box-v1.md contains an explicit Box 7 → wrong row with dueAt = nowUtc + 60d, and the SRS policy decision-table test suite has a matching case.

findings.title: Stale 'SRS8-001..016' ID range asserted in the decision-table header and WBS 0.3 while the table spans SRS8-001..028
findings.evidenceA: docs/decision-tables/srs-8-box-v1.md line 3: 'IDs SRS8-001..016 preserve the canonical summary IDs in the policy document.' — but the table actually runs SRS8-001 through SRS8-028 (lines 7-34).
findings.evidenceB: docs/wbs/memox-v6-development-wbs.md line 244 (WBS 0.3 CP): 'leitner-8-box-v1 decision rows SRS8-001..016 agree' — same stale range used as the freeze/acceptance evidence for the SRS policy.
findings.description: Documentation drift: the decision table was extended to 28 rows but two governance references still cite the historical 1..16 range. The WBS uses the stale range as the completion boundary evidence for freezing SRS Policy v1, so the acceptance evidence understates the actual row set.
findings.impact: A reader validating the frozen policy against 'rows 001..016' will not review rows 017-028 (which include half the box transitions), weakening the freeze gate and making the exhaustiveness gap in F13-001 easier to miss.
findings.rootCause: Range string not updated when rows 017-028 were appended.
findings.recommendation: Update the header note in srs-8-box-v1.md and WBS 0.3 to reflect the actual SRS8-001..028 range (after adding the missing Box 7 wrong row from F13-001).
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Both srs-8-box-v1.md line 3 and WBS 0.3 cite the actual full ID range.

findings.title: Today Dashboard docs never define consuming a Statistics/metrics-v1 summary, contradicting Study Statistics' stated contract and metrics-v1's acceptance criterion
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/today-dashboard/README.md §Cross-object contracts (lines 27-31) lists only 'Due/new/relearn counts từ Learning Progress', 'Paused session từ Study Session; Goal/Streak từ respective projections', 'Recent Deck cards từ Deck' — Study Statistics and metrics-v1 are never mentioned in any of the 8 today-dashboard files.
findings.evidenceB: docs/business/study-statistics/README.md line 32: 'Today Dashboard chỉ consume compact summary, không tự tính metric.' AND docs/business/study-statistics/metric-formulas-v1.md line 31 (Acceptance criteria): 'Goal, Streak, Today and Statistics reference metrics-v1; no surface reimplements formulas.'
findings.description: Directional inconsistency between owning documents: Study Statistics and the canonical metrics-v1 formula contract both assert that Today Dashboard consumes a Statistics-derived compact summary and must reference metrics-v1, but the Today Dashboard aggregate — which is the authoritative owner of what Today composes — never names Statistics, metrics-v1, or a compact summary anywhere. The design confirms Today does surface statistic-like values (Dashboard.jsx lines 208-217 render a mastery/time/streak 'Today strip' and line 139 says 'Goal, streak and statistics will refresh'), so a real dependency exists that Today's own spec leaves undefined.
findings.impact: An engineer implementing Today (WBS 5.7.1/7.3) has no owning-doc contract for whether Today pulls Statistics values, which formula version applies, or how insufficient/stale statistics render on Today — risking a Today surface that recomputes metrics locally (violating metrics-v1 line 31 'no surface reimplements formulas') or omits the statistics strip the design shows.
findings.rootCause: The Statistics→Today consumption contract was authored on the Statistics side only; the Today aggregate was never updated to acknowledge the dependency.
findings.recommendation: Add a Statistics/metrics-v1 entry to today-dashboard/README.md's Cross-object contracts (mirroring the Goal/Streak entries) specifying that Today consumes a read-only compact Statistics summary keyed to metrics-v1, or, if Today does NOT consume Statistics, remove the Today claim from study-statistics/README.md line 32 and metrics-v1 line 31.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: The Today↔Statistics relationship is stated identically (present-or-absent) in both today-dashboard/README.md and study-statistics/README.md + metrics-v1.

findings.title: Study Goal and Study Streak independently re-specify local-day/timezone/DST boundary rules without cross-referencing metrics-v1's canonical localDayId
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/study-goal/handle-goal-day-boundary.md §5 'Edge decision table' (lines 44-53) fully defines midnight/travel/DST/manual-clock behavior; docs/business/study-streak/handle-streak-boundary.md §3 'Boundary decision table' (lines 26-33) independently defines the same normal-midnight/DST/timezone-travel/clock-rollback matrix.
findings.evidenceB: docs/business/study-statistics/metric-formulas-v1.md line 7 defines the single canonical contract: 'localDayId: date derived once from event UTC instant using the effective IANA timezone snapshot; format YYYY-MM-DD'. docs/business/study-streak/README.md line 30 claims Streak 'Dùng local-day contract của Study Goal' yet handle-streak-boundary.md restates its own rules rather than deferring.
findings.description: Three documents (Goal boundary, Streak boundary, metrics-v1) each contain a local-day/timezone/DST specification for what should be one shared contract. Streak's README asserts reuse of Goal's contract, and metrics-v1 declares itself the canonical localDayId owner, but neither boundary file references metrics-v1's localDayId, and the two boundary decision tables are maintained in parallel prose.
findings.impact: Parallel definitions can drift (e.g. a future DST or manual-clock edge fixed in one table but not the other), producing inconsistent day bucketing between Goal completion and Streak qualification for the same event — exactly the kind of nondeterminism the WBS clock/timezone abstraction (line 565) is meant to prevent.
findings.rootCause: Boundary logic authored per-aggregate before metrics-v1 consolidated localDayId.
findings.recommendation: Make metrics-v1's localDayId the single normative source and have handle-goal-day-boundary.md and handle-streak-boundary.md reference it for the base day-id derivation, keeping only their aggregate-specific rollover/break behavior local.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Both boundary docs cite metrics-v1 localDayId as the day-id source and do not restate the derivation independently.

findings.title: Study Goal's 'distinct qualified Cards' framing is ambiguous against the literal goalProgress formula, which can double-count a Card studied in two sessions on the same day
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/study-goal/README.md line 28: 'Goal v1 đo distinct qualified Cards theo metrics-v1' and metric-formulas-v1.md line 6: 'qualifiedCard: distinct Card with a committed terminal outcome ... Mastery retries do not increase this count.'
findings.evidenceB: docs/business/study-statistics/metric-formulas-v1.md line 11: 'goalProgress = count(distinct sessionId, cardId) for qualifying sessions in the local day.'
findings.description: The prose describes the Goal unit as 'distinct qualified Cards' (implying per-Card uniqueness within the day), but the executable formula counts distinct (sessionId, cardId) pairs. If the same physical Card reaches a terminal outcome in two different qualifying sessions on the same day (e.g. a due-review session and a relearn session), it contributes 2 to goalProgress under the formula, contradicting the 'distinct Card' reading.
findings.impact: Goal completion could fire earlier than a user expects (a 20-card goal met by 15 distinct cards studied across sessions), and Streak's qualifiedCard reuse of the same term compounds the ambiguity. Two engineers can implement two different counts, both citing metrics-v1.
findings.rootCause: The plain-language unit name and the SQL-style formula were written at different altitudes without reconciling the distinct key.
findings.recommendation: Decide and state explicitly whether the Goal unit is distinct-Card-per-day or distinct-(session,Card); if the former, change the formula to count(distinct cardId); if the latter, update README.md's 'distinct qualified Cards' wording to say 'per-session card completions'.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: metrics-v1 goalProgress formula and study-goal/README.md agree on exactly one distinct key, with a golden test for the same-card-two-sessions case.

findings.title: Design statistics spec collapses 'empty' and 'insufficient' into a single state, though business requires them never to be mixed
findings.evidenceA: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/statistics.md §State matrix (lines 27-36) lists exactly 5 states: loaded, scope-switch, insufficient, loading, error — there is no distinct 'empty' (no completed study) or 'zero-valid' state.
findings.evidenceB: docs/business/study-statistics/handle-insufficient-statistics.md §2 master flow (lines 15-22) branches into three distinct states 'No activity → Empty statistics', 'Below threshold → Insufficient guidance', 'Unavailable/error → Refresh/rebuild', and its Acceptance criteria line 45: 'Zero/empty/insufficient/error không bị trộn' (must not be mixed).
findings.description: Documentation drift between the business availability-state contract and the design state matrix. Business mandates that empty (no data yet), insufficient (below sample threshold), true-zero (valid 0 value), and error are four visually/semantically distinct states that must not be conflated; the auto-generated statistics.md exposes only 'insufficient' and 'error', giving no design surface for the 'empty / no completed study, CTA Start study' state the business requires (handle-insufficient-statistics.md line 26).
findings.impact: Without a distinct empty-state design, a brand-new user with zero sessions would see an 'insufficient sample' message (implying they studied but not enough) instead of the 'you haven't studied yet, start studying' onboarding CTA the business specifies, violating the not-mixed acceptance criterion and the Start-study recovery handoff.
findings.rootCause: The design state matrix was derived from Statistics.jsx which models only a generic insufficient/loaded/error set, predating the business empty-vs-insufficient distinction.
findings.recommendation: Add distinct 'empty' (and, if applicable, 'zero-valid') states to the statistics screen state matrix and its source registry so the four availability states each render distinctly.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: statistics.md (and Statistics.jsx) render empty, insufficient, zero-valid and error as distinct states matching handle-insufficient-statistics.md §2.

findings.title: Handle Insufficient Statistics assumes per-metric 'insufficient' thresholds live in the formula version, but metrics-v1 defines a threshold rule only for one metric
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/study-statistics/handle-insufficient-statistics.md line 6: 'Threshold thuộc formula version, không hard-code trong UI' and line 10 'Mỗi metric có thể đủ/thiếu độc lập' — i.e. each metric has its own sufficiency threshold defined by the formula version.
findings.evidenceB: docs/business/study-statistics/metric-formulas-v1.md defines an insufficiency rule for only one metric: terminalAccuracy (line 21) 'If denominator is zero, state is insufficient, not 0%'. No sample-size threshold is defined for retention, activeStudyDuration, sessionCount, qualifiedCardCount or the heatmap.
findings.description: The insufficient-statistics flow delegates all sufficiency thresholds to 'the formula version' (metrics-v1), but metrics-v1 only specifies a single zero-denominator rule for terminalAccuracy. There is no threshold contract for any other metric, so the flow's core promise ('below threshold → insufficient guidance', 'show progress-to-threshold when policy allows') has nothing to reference for most metrics.
findings.impact: Implementers cannot build the 'insufficient sample' vs 'available' decision for retention/trend/heatmap metrics without inventing thresholds in the UI — the exact thing line 6 forbids — leaving sufficiency behavior undefined and untestable for the majority of the Statistics screen.
findings.rootCause: The UI flow was written to a general per-metric-threshold model that metrics-v1 never fully specified.
findings.recommendation: Either add explicit per-metric sufficiency thresholds (minimum sample sizes) to metrics-v1, or narrow handle-insufficient-statistics.md to state that only terminalAccuracy has a defined insufficiency rule in v1 and all other metrics render at any sample size.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: For every metric the insufficient flow can mark 'insufficient', metrics-v1 defines the corresponding threshold; or the flow explicitly scopes insufficiency to terminalAccuracy only.

findings.title: Design reminder screen state matrix omits business-required scheduling/failure/success states, the denied-vs-blocked distinction, and the 'Next reminder' display
findings.evidenceA: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/reminder.md §State matrix (lines 27-36) lists exactly 5 states: on, off, time-picker, permission-denied, permission-denied-expansion. Reminder.jsx (lines 20-31) renders the permission-denied state with only an 'Open Settings' CTA and no re-request path, and renders no 'Next reminder' row (grep for 'next'/'scheduling'/'success' returns nothing).
findings.evidenceB: docs/business/reminder/README.md Canonical state coverage (lines 44-48) requires 'permission prompt/denied/blocked' and 'scheduling/submitting/failure/success'; enable-study-reminder.md line 39 mandates a 'Next reminder  Tomorrow · 19:00' row and §4 lines 44-48 define distinct Scheduling/Failure/Success states; recover-reminder-permission.md §2 (lines 17-26) distinguishes 'Can request → Try again' from 'Blocked → Open system settings' as two separate recovery paths.
findings.description: Documentation-vs-design coverage gap on the Reminder screen. The business spec defines scheduling, failure ('still off / try again' and 'previous schedule still active'), success, and a persistent 'Next reminder' summary, plus a genuine two-way permission model (requestable-denied vs OS-blocked). The design state matrix collapses all of this to on/off/time-picker plus a single denied variant whose only action is 'Open Settings' — modelling every denial as OS-blocked and offering no in-app re-request, no scheduling/failure feedback, and no next-fire-time display.
findings.impact: Engineers building the Reminder screen (WBS 9.2) have no design reference for the scheduling-in-progress, schedule-failure ('still off, try again'), reschedule-failure ('previous schedule still active'), or success/next-reminder states the business requires, and would ship a permission recovery that always deep-links to system settings even when a simple in-app re-prompt is possible — degrading the first-denial UX and leaving the mandated 'Next reminder' confirmation absent.
findings.rootCause: The design state matrix was scoped to the happy-path settings toggle plus a single OS-blocked permission variant, before the business permission recovery and scheduling lifecycle were fully specified.
findings.recommendation: Extend the reminder screen state matrix and Reminder.jsx to add scheduling, schedule-failure, reschedule-failure, success/next-reminder-visible, and a distinct permission-requestable ('Try again') state separate from permission-blocked ('Open Settings'); add the 'Next reminder' summary row from enable-study-reminder.md.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: reminder.md's state matrix covers on/off, time-picker, scheduling, schedule-failure, reschedule-failure, success (with Next reminder), permission-requestable (Try again) and permission-blocked (Open Settings), matching reminder/README.md canonical coverage.

findings.title: statistics.md and reminder.md handoff notes state a 44×44 minimum touch target, contradicting the canonical --memox-touch-min: 48px token
findings.evidenceA: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/specs/statistics.md line 40 and specs/reminder.md line 40 both state 'touch targets ≥ 44×44'.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/tokens/spacing.css line 47: '--memox-touch-min: 48px;' — and specs/dashboard.md line 51 (same rebaseline, same reviewer scope) correctly states 'touch targets ≥ 48×48'.
findings.description: Documentation drift / token-fidelity defect: two of the three specs in this workstream (statistics, reminder) quote a 44×44 minimum touch target while the design system's own canonical spacing token defines the minimum as 48px, and the sibling dashboard.md spec quotes 48×48. Per AGENTS.md the design-kit token is the source of truth for visual values, so the 44×44 handoff notes are wrong. This is the same systemic 44 vs 48 issue found across the kit (prior C7-009 P1), here localized to the two specs in scope for the Show Dashboard / Statistics / Reminder flows.
findings.impact: An engineer following the statistics or reminder handoff notes literally would build 44×44 hit targets — below the 48px token and below WCAG/Material minimums — for icon buttons (e.g. the reminder time-edit MxIconButton) and segmented controls, producing an accessibility regression the token was set to prevent.
findings.rootCause: Stale 44×44 boilerplate in the spec generator/registry not updated when --memox-touch-min was set to 48px.
findings.recommendation: Correct the handoff notes in statistics.md and reminder.md to '≥ 48×48' to match --memox-touch-min, and fix the shared generator/registry so all specs emit the token value.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: statistics.md and reminder.md state ≥48×48 (or reference --memox-touch-min), consistent with the token and dashboard.md.

findings.title: Bootstrap / corrupt-store / failed-migration recovery result has no design screen or state
findings.evidenceA: docs/architecture/adr/ADR-004-local-persistence-platforms.md:22-23 'Unsupported storage capability fails at bootstrap with a typed, user-safe recovery result'; migration-policy.md:13-14 'A failed migration preserves or restores the last valid store and returns a typed recovery result'; ADR-005:14-15 'User-visible recovery copy is localized'
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../specs/settings.md and account-sync.md contain no bootstrap-failure, corrupt-store, unsupported-storage, or failed-migration recovery state; no design spec in the kit surfaces these user-safe recovery results
findings.description: Business/architecture mandates a user-facing, localized, typed recovery result for three bootstrap/data failure classes (unsupported storage, corrupt store, failed migration). No design screen or state matrix in the design kit renders any of these recovery surfaces, so the recovery UX that ADR-004/005 and migration-policy require has no implementable design home. Documentation drift between the architecture contract and the design kit's screen inventory.
findings.impact: An engineer building the Tier-1 first-learning release has no design reference for the very failure states the architecture treats as mandatory verification targets; recovery copy/layout would be improvised.
findings.rootCause: Recovery UX defined only in architecture/business prose; never propagated into the design kit's screen catalog.
findings.recommendation: Add a bootstrap/data-recovery screen (or explicit shared error-surface states) to the design kit covering unsupported-storage, corrupt-store, and failed-migration recovery, or record an explicit deferral with an owner and WBS pointer.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: A design spec/state exists (or a tracked deferral note) for each of unsupported-storage, corrupt-store, and failed-migration user-safe recovery results.

findings.title: ADR-004 restart/corrupt-recovery verification depends on Drift, which pubspec does not declare (cross-ref D1-001)
findings.evidenceA: docs/architecture/adr/ADR-004-local-persistence-platforms.md:10 'Drift is the local canonical store' and :27-29 restart/transaction-rollback/corrupted-store recovery tests
findings.evidenceB: pubspec.yaml declares neither drift nor sqlite3 (confirmed by D1-Architecture workstream: D1-001); guard-compatibility.md tracks only missing source paths, not missing pubspec deps
findings.description: The offline/restart/crash-recovery verification surface (restart, rollback, concurrent-writer, corrupted-store recovery) is all specified against Drift, but the dependency Drift itself is not yet in the manifest and no file in this recovery/persistence set names an owner/plan to close that gap. Documentation drift between the Accepted ADR and the actual dependency baseline.
findings.impact: None of the ADR-004 restart/recovery tests can be authored until the dependency is added; the gap is untracked in the persistence/recovery docs.
findings.rootCause: ADR marked Accepted ahead of the dependency-baseline WBS item; no back-reference.
findings.recommendation: Reference this against WBS 1.1 (Dependency baseline) explicitly, or add a tracked activation-debt item; primary fix owned by D1-001.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: pubspec baseline (WBS 1.1) is cross-linked from the persistence/migration contract as the owner of adding drift before restart/recovery tests.

findings.flow: Account lifecycle (sign-in / sync / conflict / recovery / sign-out / delete)
findings.title: Design sync-conflict state auto-merges 'last-write-wins', contradicting business 'no default cloud-wins' + mandatory explicit resolution
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/account/resolve-sync-conflict.md:6 'Không có default cloud-wins' and :10 'Deck parent/content type, Card content, Progress, Attempts, Session/SRS history bắt buộc explicit whole-record resolution'; account/README.md:11 'Một conflict cần explicit resolution policy; không mặc định cloud thắng'; sync-local-data.md:10 'Conflict không được auto cloud-wins nếu policy chưa rõ'
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/MemoX Design System_v4/ui_kits/memox-app/_features/account-sync/components/SyncBlock.jsx:28-29 conflict state renders window.ListRow title="Merged" sub="Kept the latest (last-write-wins)" and window.Note text="Your devices' data was merged safely." — an automatic last-write-wins resolution with no user decision
findings.description: The design kit's account-sync conflict state depicts conflicts as already auto-resolved via last-write-wins ('Merged / Kept the latest / merged safely'). Business explicitly forbids any default cloud-wins/auto-merge and requires explicit whole-record user resolution for structural, Card content, Progress, Attempts and Session/SRS history. This is a direct Business-vs-Design contradiction on conflict-resolution semantics — a data-integrity behavior, not a cosmetic one.
findings.impact: If built to the mock, sync would silently overwrite the losing side's learning Progress/Attempts/SRS history with last-write-wins, causing irreversible data loss and violating the aggregate invariants. This is the exact failure the business contract was written to prevent.
findings.rootCause: Design mock modeled the happy 'already synced' path with a simplistic merge label; never reconciled with the business conflict-resolution contract.
findings.recommendation: Product Owner must decide the canonical conflict behavior. Business is source of truth for domain behavior (per AGENTS.md); the design conflict state must be reworked into the Compare/choose/merge decision surface resolve-sync-conflict.md mandates, or business must be amended. Do not ship last-write-wins auto-merge.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Design conflict state presents object/changed-fields/versions and an explicit keep-local/use-remote/merge decision with no automatic last-write-wins, matching resolve-sync-conflict.md; or a recorded PO decision changes the business contract.

findings.flow: Account lifecycle (sign-in / sync / conflict / recovery / sign-out / delete)
findings.title: Design Account screen ships live Google + email/password sign-in CTAs, pre-committing a provider the cloud-service gate defers
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/account/cloud-service-gate.md:8-9 'No implementation may infer a provider from a UI mock or generic architecture note', :26-27 'Until the gate is accepted, cloud CTAs are absent or explicitly labelled unavailable', and :33-34 'The provider decision is intentionally not made in this document'
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../specs/account-sync.md:12 'Primary CTA: **Sign in**'; AccountSync.jsx / SignInCard.jsx render a functional primary MxButton 'Sign in with Google' plus EmailSignInForm with real email + password AuthFields and 'Continue with email' — a concrete Google + email/password provider commitment with live (not disabled) CTAs
findings.description: The design kit presents a fully realized Google + email/password sign-in as the account screen's primary action. The business cloud-service gate is deferred for v1 and requires that until accepted, no provider be inferred from a mock and cloud CTAs be absent or explicitly labelled unavailable. The mock adds an 'ALPHA' badge and 'This feature is in alpha' caption, but the CTAs are functional primary actions, not 'unavailable' — so the fail-closed rule is not satisfied. Confirms and extends B3-001.
findings.impact: Building to the mock pre-selects Google + email/password before Product/Security record the mandated provider/security/retention decision, exactly the outcome the gate forbids; it also implies credential handling (password field) with no accepted storage decision.
findings.rootCause: Design mock built a complete cloud sign-in ahead of the deferred provider gate; the 'alpha' label was treated as sufficient fail-closed mitigation.
findings.recommendation: Product/Security must either accept the cloud-service gate (recording provider, auth mechanisms, credential storage, etc.) or the design must render cloud CTAs as absent/labelled-unavailable per the fail-closed rule. PO decision required; do not treat the mock as the provider decision.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Either an accepted cloud-service-gate decision record exists, or the design account screen shows no functional cloud sign-in CTA (absent or explicitly disabled/unavailable) for the Tier-1 release.

findings.flow: Account lifecycle (sign-in / sync / conflict / recovery / sign-out / delete)
findings.title: account/README labels Sign-in/Sync/Conflict/Recover flows unconditionally 'Đã có' despite Account+Sync being a deferred conditional capability
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/account/README.md:18-22 lists sign-in, sign-out, sync-local-data, resolve-sync-conflict, recover-account-session with status 'Đã có' (present) and no conditional flag, while :23-24 flag only delete-account ('Đã có — Conditional') and cloud-service-gate ('Deferred for v1')
findings.evidenceB: docs/business/account/cloud-service-gate.md:7-9 'Account, ongoing sync and cloud backup are conditional capabilities. Web + Android v1 may ship the local-first learning flow without them.'
findings.description: The account flow catalog marks the core sign-in/sync/conflict/recover specs as unconditionally present while the cloud-service gate (and WBS §14 which starts 'only after local workflows... are stable') classifies the whole Account+Sync family as conditional/deferred. A reader cannot tell from README whether these flows are in-scope for Tier-1 or gated behind the deferred provider decision. Aligns with B3-002 (which rated it P1).
findings.impact: Ambiguous scope status risks a team treating gated sync flows as v1-ready, or conversely deprioritizing them, without a single authoritative statement.
findings.rootCause: 'Đã có' conflates 'spec written' with 'in scope'; the conditional-gate status was applied only to two of seven rows.
findings.recommendation: Add a conditional/deferred marker (consistent with delete-account and cloud-service-gate) to the sign-in/sync/conflict/recover rows, cross-referencing the cloud-service gate and WBS §14 sequencing.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: README status column consistently reflects that Account+Sync flows are conditional on the deferred cloud-service gate.

findings.flow: Account lifecycle (sign-in / sync / conflict / recovery / sign-out / delete)
findings.title: Design account-sync state matrix omits business-mandated distinct sync states (idle, success, error)
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/account/README.md:9 'Sync status rõ: signed-out, idle, syncing, success, offline, error hoặc conflict'; sync-local-data.md:30 'Signed-out, idle, syncing, success, offline, error và conflict là distinct states' and :52 'UI phân biệt offline/error/conflict'
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../specs/account-sync.md:27-38 state matrix lists signed-out, signed-in, syncing, conflict, offline, sign-in-form, sign-in-keyboard, expansion — no distinct idle, success, or error state (SyncBlock.jsx collapses success into a 'Synced' row and has no error branch)
findings.description: Business defines seven distinct sync states and explicitly requires the UI to distinguish offline vs error vs conflict. The design account-sync spec/component covers syncing/offline/conflict but has no distinct error state and no idle-vs-signed-in distinction; a sync error would have nowhere to render. Documentation/design coverage gap against the business state contract.
findings.impact: Sync error handling (auth error vs network error vs data conflict, which README L31 requires distinguishing) has no design surface, so error UX would be improvised or omitted.
findings.rootCause: Design modeled the sync-status affordance around success/offline/conflict and never added the error state the business contract enumerates.
findings.recommendation: Add distinct error (and, if the state machine needs it, idle/success) states to the account-sync design spec and SyncBlock component matching the business state contract.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Design account-sync matrix includes distinct error (and idle/success as needed) states and distinguishes offline/error/conflict.

findings.flow: Account lifecycle (sign-in / sync / conflict / recovery / sign-out / delete)
findings.title: Business sign-out Confirmation flow (pending-sync review + confirm + double-confirm guard) has no design state; design renders a bare destructive sign-out button
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/account/sign-out.md:14-23 master flow requires 'Inspect pending sync' → 'Confirm local data stays' → confirm/cancel/signing-out/failure/success; :44-49 acceptance 'Pending sync impact được trình bày' and 'Double confirm không gây side effect lặp'
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../AccountSync.jsx renders MxButton variant=ghost danger 'Sign out' (node account/signout) directly with no confirmation step, and account-sync.md:27-38 state matrix has no sign-out-confirm / signing-out / sign-out-failure state
findings.description: Sign-out is a Confirmation archetype in business with a mandatory pending-sync review, confirm/cancel, and idempotent double-confirm guard. The design provides only a single destructive button with none of those states in its matrix. Design coverage gap for a specified flow.
findings.impact: A destructive sign-out with no confirmation/pending-sync review contradicts the business acceptance criteria and risks users losing pending sync context or triggering repeated revoke side effects.
findings.rootCause: Design collapsed the whole Account+Sync family into one screen and modeled sign-out as a single button, dropping the confirmation flow's states.
findings.recommendation: Add sign-out confirmation states (pending-sync review, confirm/cancel, signing-out, failure/success) to the design, or reference the confirmation via a shared dialog composite.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Design covers a sign-out confirmation with pending-sync review and no-duplicate-side-effect guard, matching sign-out.md.

findings.flow: Account lifecycle (sign-in / sync / conflict / recovery / sign-out / delete)
findings.title: Recover-account-session (re-auth-required banner, expired/revoked states, 'Continue offline' CTA) has no design surface
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/account/recover-account-session.md:14-24 master flow (session invalid → mark re-auth required, pause sync → Sign in again / Continue offline / Sign out); :29-31 'Archetype: Recoverable account banner/screen. Primary CTA Sign in again; Continue offline luôn rõ'; :40-44 states expired/revoked/wrong-account
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../specs/account-sync.md:27-38 state matrix and AccountSync.jsx contain no re-auth-required / session-expired / continue-offline state or component
findings.description: The account-session recovery flow (its own Recoverable-banner archetype with expired/revoked/wrong-account states and a 'Continue offline' CTA) has no corresponding design state or surface. Design coverage gap for a specified recovery flow that is central to offline/account resilience.
findings.impact: When credentials expire mid-use, there is no design reference for the re-auth banner or the 'continue offline' path, both of which the business marks mandatory for keeping local Study usable.
findings.rootCause: Recovery flow specified in business but never given a design state.
findings.recommendation: Add a re-auth-required recovery state/banner (with Continue offline / Sign in again / Sign out) to the design kit, or a dedicated recovery spec.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Design includes a re-auth-required recovery surface with expired/revoked states and a Continue-offline CTA matching recover-account-session.md.

findings.flow: Account lifecycle (sign-in / sync / conflict / recovery / sign-out / delete)
findings.title: Resolve-sync-conflict Compare/decision archetype collapsed to a single non-interactive status row in design
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/account/resolve-sync-conflict.md:15-27 master flow (load local+remote+base → compare fields/impact → choose keep-local/use-remote/merge → revalidate → atomic apply); :32-33 'Archetype: Compare/decision. Primary CTA Apply resolution'
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../specs/account-sync.md:27-38 exposes conflict as one of eight states on a single account screen; SyncBlock.jsx:25-29 conflict renders a static 'Merged / last-write-wins' status row with no compare/choose/merge UI
findings.description: Business defines conflict resolution as its own Compare/decision screen with side-by-side local/remote/base comparison and an explicit Apply-resolution CTA. Design represents it as a single passive status row. Beyond the P0 last-write-wins contradiction (F22-001), the entire comparison/decision surface is missing. Design coverage gap.
findings.impact: Even if the auto-merge is corrected (F22-001), there is no design surface for the mandated compare/choose/merge interaction, so the conflict-resolution UX cannot be built from the kit.
findings.rootCause: Design consolidated six business flows into one screen; the conflict compare/decision archetype lost its dedicated surface.
findings.recommendation: Provide a dedicated conflict-resolution Compare/decision screen in the design kit (fields, timestamps/versions, keep/use/merge choices, Apply resolution), separate from the account status row.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Design kit includes a Compare/decision conflict-resolution surface matching resolve-sync-conflict.md's master flow and CTA.

findings.flow: Account lifecycle (sign-in / sync / conflict / recovery / sign-out / delete)
findings.title: Delete-account conditional flow has no design surface (acceptable while gated, but note traceability)
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/account/delete-account.md:5-9 'Product scope: chưa xác nhận... Không expose entry point cho đến khi policy backend, retention, legal và local-data choice được duyệt'; WBS memox-v6-development-wbs.md:501 '14.5 Account deletion conditional flow ... Only if product support is approved'
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../AccountSync.jsx and account-sync.md contain no delete-account entry, state, or screen
findings.description: Delete-account is intentionally product-unconfirmed and its entry point must stay hidden until dependencies are approved; the absence of a design surface is therefore correct, not a defect. Flagged only for traceability: the business spec and WBS 14.5 are consistent, and the design correctly omits an entry point. No conflict.
findings.impact: Minimal — this is the intended fail-closed behavior; noted so the audit chain shows the omission is deliberate and traced.
findings.rootCause: Correctly deferred capability; noted for completeness.
findings.recommendation: No action needed now; when WBS 14.5 becomes Ready, add the conditional delete-account design surface per delete-account.md acceptance criteria.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: When product support is approved, a delete-account design surface exists with hidden-until-eligible entry and re-auth + explicit confirm.

findings.title: Search filters by SRS learning status in design but by object type/language/deck/visibility in business, and surfaces Progress data Search says it does not index
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/search/filter-search-results.md:1 — "Flow này lọc result theo object type, language pair, Deck scope và visibility trong phạm vi policy Search hỗ trợ." Reinforced by docs/business/search/README.md:38 ("Statistics/Progress content không được index như Card text nếu chưa có explicit scope") and docs/business/search/update-search-index.md §3 ("Không index Progress/Account/private fields ngoài explicit scope").
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../_features/search/components/Chips.jsx: `const FILTERS = ['All', 'New', 'Due', 'Mastered'];` — the only filter row in Search.jsx (states `filtered`/`loading`/`no-results` all render `<Chips>`). ResultRow also renders a per-result `status: 'due'|'mastered'|'new'` badge (Search.jsx:8-12).
findings.description: The design's only filter affordance filters by Leitner/SRS learning status (New/Due/Mastered), which is Learning-Progress state. The business Search spec defines filters along completely different dimensions (object type, language pair, deck scope, visibility) and explicitly states Search does not index Progress content without explicit scope. The business-defined filter dimensions have no design surface at all, and the design's status filter has no business owner. This is a business-vs-design disagreement on Search capability/scope.
findings.impact: Engineers cannot reconcile which filter set to build. Building the design's status filter would require Search to index Progress, violating the business index contract; building the business filters leaves the design's entire filter UI unspecified.
findings.rootCause: Design filter chips were modeled on SRS status without cross-checking the business filter contract and the no-Progress-indexing invariant.
findings.recommendation: Product Owner must decide the canonical Search filter dimension set: either (a) adopt business dimensions (type/language/deck/visibility) and redesign the chip row + add a filter sheet, or (b) expand the Search index scope to include Progress status and update update-search-index.md/README index-scope invariants accordingly.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Business filter dimensions and design filter chips name the same filter set, and the Search index-scope invariant is consistent with whatever filter data is surfaced.

findings.title: Design Search has no error / stale-index / rebuild state despite a dedicated business recovery flow
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/search/recover-search-failure.md §2 master flow (Temporary/Stale/Corrupt-gap → Rebuild) and §5 state matrix: "Temporary failure, offline, stale, corrupt, rebuilding"; README.md canonical states include "error".
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../specs/search.md §State matrix — "5 states": empty-recent, results, filtered, no-results, loading. No error/stale/rebuild state; Search.jsx has no error branch.
findings.description: The business Search family includes a whole flow (recover-search-failure.md) for query failure, stale/corrupt index, and rebuild handoff, plus offline behavior, requiring the UI to distinguish no-results from failure. The design Search screen models only success-path states and has no error, offline, stale-index, or rebuilding surface.
findings.impact: A Flutter engineer porting the Search screen from the design kit will have no reference for the error/stale/rebuild states, and the acceptance criterion "UI phân biệt no-results với failure" has no design representation to verify against.
findings.recommendation: Add error/stale/rebuilding states to the Search design spec and source, or explicitly annotate that these are deferred to a later WBS item with a pointer.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Search design spec enumerates error/stale/rebuild states matching recover-search-failure.md's state matrix.

findings.title: Reset-progress confirm dialog copy, safe-action label and scope framing conflict between business and design
findings.evidenceA:
docs/business/deck/reset-deck-progress.md:43-52 mandates the dialog copy: "Reset learning progress?

This will reset progress for <affected count> cards in “<Deck name>”.
Your cards and nested decks will stay in place.

Keep progress   Reset progress" with §1 "Safe action `Keep progress`; destructive action `Reset progress`", §1 "Copy phải phân biệt Reset progress với Delete Deck", and §4 line 52 "Parent bổ sung `<descendant count> nested decks` trong impact summary".
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../_shared/DeckResetConfirmDialog.jsx:8-14 — title="Reset progress?", text="Reset every card in this deck back to New? Their review boxes and due dates will be cleared.", buttons: ghost "Cancel" and primary/danger "Reset".
findings.description: The design confirm dialog diverges from the business-mandated dialog on three points: (1) safe-action label is "Cancel" vs business-required "Keep progress"; destructive label is "Reset" vs "Reset progress"; (2) body copy scopes to "every card in this deck" whereas business scopes to "<affected count> cards" (only cards that have progress) and requires the reassurance "Your cards and nested decks will stay in place"; (3) the design provides no affected-count or parent descendant-count token, which business mandates.
findings.impact: The confirm dialog is the destructive-safety gate for an irreversible progress wipe. Divergent labels/copy defeat the business requirement that the safe path be clearly labeled and that Reset be distinguishable from Delete, and the "every card" framing overstates scope relative to the count-based business contract.
findings.rootCause: Design confirm dialog was authored as a generic static stub without binding to the business copy contract or count model.
findings.recommendation: Product Owner to confirm business copy as canonical (labels Keep progress / Reset progress, affected-count-based body, nested-deck reassurance, parent descendant count) and update DeckResetConfirmDialog to templated copy; or explicitly ratify the simplified design copy and amend reset-deck-progress.md §1/§4.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Design dialog labels and copy match the mandated business strings and support affected-count / parent-descendant-count substitution.

findings.title: Design reset flow is a single static confirm dialog missing the business-required state/variant matrix
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/deck/reset-deck-progress.md §7 Lifecycle (loading impact, nothing-to-reset, resetting, failure, success), §10 State matrix (Leaf/Parent/Empty, impact loading/changed, active-session blocked, resetting/failure/success/not-found), §11 Action matrix; learning-progress/reset-learning-progress.md §5 decision table (active session intersects → block).
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../_shared/DeckResetConfirmDialog.jsx renders one fixed dialog with no props; docs/design/.../_features/deck-settings/DeckSettings.jsx:40 wires a single `reset-confirm` state to it (no count injection, no parent/empty/active-session/resetting/failure/success variants).
findings.description: Documentation drift/gap: the business reset flow specifies a rich state matrix (impact-loading, nothing-to-reset, parent aggregate, active-session-blocked, resetting, failure/rollback, success) but the only design representation is a single hardcoded confirmation dialog. None of the non-happy-path states have a design surface.
findings.impact: An engineer implementing reset from the design kit has no reference for the impact-count display, the empty/no-progress no-op, the active-session block, or the in-progress/failure/success surfaces, all of which are safety-critical for a destructive operation.
findings.recommendation: Extend the reset design (dialog + follow-on states) to cover impact-loading, nothing-to-reset, parent-descendant-count, active-session-blocked, resetting, failure/rollback and success, or annotate them as deferred with a WBS pointer.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Reset design covers each state named in reset-deck-progress.md §10 state matrix and §11 action matrix.

findings.title: Entire Backup/Restore business flow family has no design surface while Settings advertises a Backup/Restore entry
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/backup/README.md flow catalog lists seven owned flows all marked "Đã có" (create-local-backup, inspect-backup, restore-local-backup, handle-backup-compatibility, recover-backup-failure, manage-cloud-backup, backup-format-v1); restore-local-backup.md and recover-backup-failure.md specify destructive/rollback UI states.
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../_features/settings/Settings.jsx:138 renders a row `title="Backup / Restore" sub="Local file · on device" node="settings/backup"`, but there is no `specs/backup*.md`, no `_features/backup*` directory, and a repo-wide grep of the kit finds no backup/restore screen (only the Settings row, a Library import button, and unrelated 'restore focus' comments).
findings.description: The Settings screen advertises a "Backup / Restore" destination, but the entire Backup/Restore flow family (create, inspect, restore with Merge/Replace, compatibility handling, failure recovery) has no design screen, state matrix, or component in the design kit. This is a coverage gap between a shipped entry point and the missing destination.
findings.impact: The most data-critical, destructive flow in the app (full-database restore with rollback) has zero visual/interaction reference for a Flutter port, and the Settings entry point dead-ends. Backup is a downstream WBS wave (15.x, depends on 13.4), so partial deferral may be intentional, but the advertised entry point and the business "Đã có" status overstate readiness.
findings.rootCause: Backup design was not built while the Settings hub was, leaving an entry point without a destination.
findings.recommendation: Either add design screens/state matrices for at least create, inspect and restore (Merge/Replace + rollback), or mark the Settings "Backup / Restore" row and the backup README statuses as design-pending with an explicit WBS 15.x pointer so readiness is not overstated.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Either backup/restore design screens exist for create/inspect/restore, or the entry point and business statuses are annotated as design-pending with a WBS reference.

findings.title: Export design lets a content export include Leitner box + due date (Progress), which the content-transfer format contract forbids
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/content-transfer/formats-v1.md:14 — "Neither format includes Learning Progress, Attempts, Sessions, Preferences, Account tokens or device permissions. Full recovery uses [Backup format v1]." docs/business/content-transfer/configure-export.md:11 — "Content export không được gọi là Backup nếu thiếu progress/settings/history" (content export is deliberately NOT a backup and does not carry progress).
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../_features/export/Export.jsx:63-66 — config state renders a card `title="Include review state" sub="Leitner box + due date"` with a switch `node="export/incl-srs-switch"` defaulting checked (`useState(true)`).
findings.description: The design export screen offers a toggle to include "review state (Leitner box + due date)" in the exported file, defaulted ON. Business content-transfer formats (memox-csv-v1 and memox-json-v1) categorically exclude Learning Progress; carrying SRS box/due date is the defining property of Backup format v1, not content transfer. The design blurs the business's explicit Backup-vs-content-transfer boundary.
findings.impact: Implementing the toggle would produce an export file that violates the format contract and confuses users into thinking a content export is a full backup, defeating the invariant that only Backup carries progress. It also has data-safety implications: users may rely on a content export for recovery and lose progress.
findings.rootCause: Design export conflated content transfer with backup, adding a progress-inclusion option the format spec excludes.
findings.recommendation: Product Owner to decide: remove the "Include review state" toggle from content export (business as-is), or expand the content-transfer format contract to optionally carry Progress and reconcile with Backup ownership. Do not implement the toggle until resolved.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Export options and the format contract agree on whether Learning Progress can be included in a content export.

findings.title: Import/Export design offers Excel (.xlsx) and clipboard and omits JSON, conflicting with the two-format (csv/json) business contract
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/content-transfer/formats-v1.md:5-8 defines exactly two supported formats: `memox-csv-v1` and `memox-json-v1` (JSON is the only Hierarchy=Yes format). docs/business/content-transfer/configure-export.md:51 — "Format selection chỉ gồm `memox-csv-v1` và `memox-json-v1` theo formats-v1".
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../_features/export/components/FormatList.jsx — `FORMATS = [CSV .csv, Excel .xlsx, Copy text (clipboard)]`; docs/design/.../_features/import/Import.jsx:8-12 — `SOURCES = [CSV file, Excel .xlsx, Paste text]`. Neither offers JSON.
findings.description: Both the export format list and the import source list expose Excel (.xlsx) as a first-class format and (for export) a copy-to-clipboard option, while neither offers the JSON format. Business supports only memox-csv-v1 and memox-json-v1, with JSON being the sole hierarchy-preserving format. The design adds an unsupported format (Excel), adds an unspecified transport (clipboard), and drops the only hierarchy-capable format (JSON).
findings.impact: The design promises Excel import/export that the business scope excludes (requiring an .xlsx parser/serializer not in the format spec), and provides no way to do a hierarchy-preserving export/import at all, since JSON is absent. Engineers cannot build to a coherent format set.
findings.rootCause: Design format lists were populated with common file types rather than the business format contract.
findings.recommendation: Product Owner to decide the canonical transfer format set: keep business (csv + json, add JSON to the design, drop/redefine Excel and clipboard) or expand formats-v1 to include xlsx/clipboard with defined schema and hierarchy behavior.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Design import sources and export formats enumerate exactly the business-supported format set, including a hierarchy-preserving option.

findings.title: Import duplicate handling in design is a single bulk 'import anyway?' callout; business requires Skip/Merge/Import-anyway with incoming-vs-existing comparison
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/content-transfer/resolve-import-duplicates.md:1-3,§2 master flow (Skip / Merge supported / Import anyway → Keep-both), §3 "Incoming/existing content và Deck path phải phân biệt rõ", §6 "Merge tuân Flashcard invariants".
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../_features/import/Import.jsx:113-115 — `dup-warning` renders one `<window.ActionCallout icon="warning" text="8 cards already exist — import anyway?">`; the only follow-on action is the shared "Import 124 cards" button. No Skip, no Merge, no per-item choice, no side-by-side compare.
findings.description: The business duplicate-resolution flow mandates a per-item or bulk decision of Skip / Merge / Import-anyway (keep-both) with an incoming-vs-existing comparison and deck path disambiguation. The design collapses this to a single bulk warning banner offering only 'import anyway', with no Skip, no Merge, and no comparison surface.
findings.impact: Users cannot skip or merge duplicates, and merge (which must follow the Flashcard merge contract) has no design representation, risking unintended duplicates or overwrites. The commit acceptance "no overwrite without explicit decision" cannot be honored by the design.
findings.rootCause: Design modeled duplicates as a single warning rather than the business decision set.
findings.recommendation: Product Owner to decide the duplicate-resolution UX scope; if business stands, redesign the dup state into a comparison/resolution list with Skip/Merge/Import-anyway per candidate (and bulk).
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Import design provides Skip/Merge/Import-anyway decisions with incoming-vs-existing comparison matching resolve-import-duplicates.md.

findings.title: Import preview shows only a total count; business requires valid/invalid/skipped/duplicate breakdown, invalid-row reasons and hierarchy preview
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/content-transfer/preview-import.md §1 "Valid/invalid/skipped/duplicate candidates có counts riêng", "Invalid row có reason và source location đủ sửa", "Hierarchy preview không được làm phẳng âm thầm"; §6 "Invalid rows không bị commit âm thầm".
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../_features/import/Import.jsx:116-118 — preview renders only `SectionLabel "PREVIEW · 124 CARDS"` plus a flat `<Table rows={ROWS}>` and an "Import 124 cards" button. No per-status counts, no invalid-row surface, no tree/hierarchy preview.
findings.description: The design import preview surfaces only a single total-card count and a flat sample table. The business preview requires separate valid/invalid/skipped/duplicate counts, invalid rows with reason and source location, and a hierarchy (tree) preview that is not silently flattened.
findings.impact: Users cannot see which rows are invalid or duplicated before committing, and hierarchical imports (JSON) have no tree preview, undermining the "invalid rows not silently committed" acceptance criterion.
findings.recommendation: Add per-status count summary, invalid-row detail, and a hierarchy/tree preview to the import preview design.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Import preview design shows valid/invalid/skipped/duplicate counts, invalid-row reasons, and a hierarchy preview.

findings.title: Export config lacks the business-required pre-export count summary and CSV flatten warning
findings.evidenceA: docs/business/content-transfer/configure-export.md §2 master flow step G "Review counts/hierarchy behavior", §6 "Plan nêu rõ scope, format và hierarchy behavior", and line 51 "hierarchy với CSV bắt buộc explicit flatten warning".
findings.evidenceB: docs/design/.../_features/export/Export.jsx:49-69 — the `config` state shows SCOPE / FORMAT / SEPARATOR / include-review-state and an Export button, but no scope card/card counts and no flatten warning when a hierarchical (subtree) scope is exported to a flat CSV.
findings.description: Business requires the export plan to present scope/counts and, specifically, an explicit flatten warning when a hierarchical scope is exported to CSV (which cannot preserve hierarchy). The design export config shows neither a count summary nor any flatten warning, even though it offers an "Incl. sub-decks" scope alongside a flat CSV format.
findings.impact: Users exporting a parent/subtree to CSV get no warning that hierarchy will be flattened and no count review, contradicting the configure-export acceptance criteria.
findings.recommendation: Add a scope/count summary and a conditional CSV flatten warning to the export config design.
findings.acceptanceCriteria: Export config design displays scope counts and shows an explicit flatten warning for hierarchical-scope CSV exports.

resolutions.summary: Language-pair removal: no cascade delete; block on dependency. Design reconciled (RemoveLanguageDialog.jsx blocked dialog + fixed confirm copy, Languages.jsx remove-blocked state, languages.md state matrix).

Sync conflict: no default cloud-wins / no last-write-wins; explicit resolution required. Design reconciled (SyncBlock.jsx conflict state routes to Compare/decision, account-sync.md state matrix annotated).
